confirmed.title: Big file shrinking below threshold is silently recorded as deleted; restoring that checkpoint deletes the file from disk
confirmed.description: When a CAS-managed big file shrinks below blob_threshold_mb (truncating a log, replacing a 60MB asset with a 10MB one — no crash needed), scan_changes demotes it with needs_add=false. The shrunken file is never `git add`ed and pointer paths are never left in the index, so write-tree omits the path entirely: the checkpoint records the file as DELETED while it sits on disk. The new (small) content is captured nowhere — not in git, not in CAS. A subsequent `asp restore` to that checkpoint then runs the diff-filter=D deletion pass and removes the file from disk. Verified end-to-end: cp1 has 2MB data.log; shrink it to 2KB; cp2's tree contains only app.py; `asp restore 2` (restoring the checkpoint just taken, tree untouched) deletes data.log. This is silent data loss in the flagship 'every checkpoint recoverable' flow. It also masks the kill-9-mid-restore residue case: a leftover 104-byte pointer file is likewise recorded as a deletion. The next checkpoint after cp2 re-captures the file as untracked, so exactly one checkpoint in the timeline is permanently wrong — but restore through it is destructive.
confirmed.suggested_fix: In the demote/delete arm of scan_changes, distinguish the two cases: if the path still exists as a file (shrunk below threshold), push `(path, true)` so it is staged as a normal git blob (excludes are already regenerated without it, so `git add` will pick it up); push `(path, false)` only when the file is actually gone. Add an engine test: big->small shrink, assert the path is in the checkpoint tree with the new content and survives a full restore round-trip.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: Confirmed end-to-end with the release binary (target/release/asp) in /tmp. Repro: threshold 1MB; cp1 = {app.py, data.log(2MB)} captures data.log via CAS pointer correctly. Shrink data.log to 2KB; `asp checkpoint -m cp2` succeeds ("1 file(s)") but `git ls-tree -r refs/asp/checkpoints/2` contains ONLY app.py — the present, modified file is recorded as deleted, and the 2KB content exists in neither git objects nor .asp/blobs (verified by a cat-file --batch-all-objects sweep + CAS grep). `asp restore 2` then deletes data.log from disk via the diff-filter=D pass. Code path: crates/asp-core/src/workspace.rs:596-600 demotes a shrunk big file with needs_add=false; stage_tree (workspace.rs:456-460) only stages needs_add=true paths; the file is invisible to git status at that point because excludes are rewritten only after scan_changes (lines 436-441) and the pointer was removed from the index after the prior write-tree (lines 509-518); restore's deletion pass is workspace.rs:741-757. Permanent-loss variant verified: if the shrunken content changes before the next checkpoint, the cp2-era content is unrecoverable from any checkpoint or CAS blob. Mitigations are partial only: restore takes an auto safety checkpoint that captures restore-time (not checkpoint-time) content, and the next checkpoint re-captures the file as untracked — exactly one checkpoint is permanently wrong and destructive to restore through. No test coverage: the only big-file test (crates/asp-core/tests/engine.rs:403, big_files_go_to_cas_sidecar_and_restore) grows 2MB→3MB and never crosses the threshold downward. The trigger is ordinary (truncating a log, replacing a large asset with a smaller one), the failure is silent, and it violates the core "every checkpoint recoverable" trust model in the flagship checkpoint/restore flow. The suggested fix (push (path, true) when the path still exists as a file, (path, false) only when gone) is sound: excludes are regenerated without the path before the git add call, so the shrunken file would be staged as a normal blob. Release-blocking for v0.1.0; severity critical stands.

confirmed.title: promote commits the fork's entire .asp store (shadow git, journal, CAS blobs, lock) into the user's branch
confirmed.description: build_user_commit stages the fork with `git add -A .` against the user's repo with only the user's .gitignore in effect. Nobody gitignores `.asp/`, so the promoted branch contains the complete sidecar store: shadow.git objects (every checkpoint of every file the agent ever touched), journal.jsonl (full provenance log), .asp/blobs/* (multi-GB CAS originals committed as ordinary git blobs), shadow.index and the lock file. Consequences: (1) the user repo balloons — a 1GB CAS blob becomes a 1GB git object in their repo the moment they promote; (2) pushing the branch publishes their entire private local edit history (checkpoints capture files the user never committed, e.g. scratch notes/keys in non-gitignored files); (3) checking out or merging the branch tries to write `.asp/` into the live workspace root, colliding with the real store ('untracked working tree files would be overwritten'). This contradicts the trust model ('promote creating ordinary branches') and the brief's 'fully-reviewable' claim — the PR diff is unreviewable noise. The existing promote test asserts the branch contains src/new_module.rs but never asserts `.asp` is absent (crates/asp-core/tests/engine.rs:249-305).
confirmed.evidence:
Repro: init git repo + asp; fork; edit; `asp promote winner`; then `git ls-tree -r --name-only asp/winner` lists 31 .asp/ paths including .asp/shadow.git/objects/*, .asp/journal.jsonl, .asp/lock, .asp/shadow.index, .asp/shadow.git/refs/asp/checkpoints/1.
Code (build_user_commit): `run(&["add", "-A", "."], false)?;` — no exclusion of ASP_DIR.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Exclude the store from the staged tree: `run(&["add", "-A", "--", ".", ":(exclude).asp"], false)` (pathspec magic works with a temp index and requires no .gitignore changes). Extend promote_lands_branch_in_user_repo with `assert!(!tree.contains(".asp"))`.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning:
CONFIRMED by full reproduction with target/release/asp v0.1.0. Attempted to refute; every element of the claim held up.

Code evidence: /Users/apple/Projects/agentspaces/crates/asp-core/src/workspace.rs:1566 — build_user_commit runs `run(&["add", "-A", "."], false)?` against the fork through a temp index with no exclusion of ASP_DIR. Nothing mitigates this: the `/.asp/` exclude in config.rs only goes into the SHADOW repo's info/exclude (workspace.rs:229); Workspace::init (workspace.rs:183-255) never writes `.asp/` into the user's .gitignore or .git/info/exclude; no doc (README.md, docs/design/format.md, v1-brief.md) ever tells users to gitignore `.asp/`. So the default path for every git user includes the store.

Reproduction (fresh git repo in /tmp, asp init, fork --name winner, edit, checkpoint, promote winner):
1. `git ls-tree -r --name-only asp/winner` listed 36 `.asp/` paths: shadow.git/objects/*, shadow.git/refs/asp/checkpoints/*, journal.jsonl, lock, shadow.index, workspace.json, config.toml, forks.json, format-version, and all shadow.git hook samples.
2. Merge collision: `git merge asp/winner` — the exact command asp's own success message prints ("merge it: git merge asp/winner") — fails 100% of the time with "untracked working tree files would be overwritten by merge" listing every .asp path, because the live workspace always has its own untracked `.asp/`. The headline promote->merge workflow is unconditionally broken.
3. Privacy leak: a never-committed file (notes.txt containing "secret-scratch-note-API-KEY-12345", present before `asp init`, never `git add`ed by the user) was fully recoverable from the promoted branch via the embedded shadow store: `git --git-dir=<worktree>/.asp/shadow.git show refs/asp/checkpoints/1:notes.txt` printed the secret. Pushing the branch publishes the user's entire local checkpoint history.
4. Repo balloon: a 60MB file in a fork landed in the fork's CAS (.asp/blobs/<sha256>) and promote committed that CAS file as an ordinary git blob (`git ls-tree -r asp/bigone -- .asp/blobs/` shows it); the user's .git grew to 61M. The large-blob sidecar design (keep big files OUT of git) is defeated by promote.

Docs contradiction confirmed: CLAUDE.md:30 ("user's own .git is sacred — never write to it except promote creating ordinary branches") and docs/design/v1-brief.md:7 ("fully-reviewable fork") / :45 ("winner lands as an ordinary git branch/PR"). Test gap confirmed: crates/asp-core/tests/engine.rs:249-305 (promote_lands_branch_in_user_repo) asserts the branch contains src/new_module.rs but never asserts `.asp` is absent.

Suggested fix is sound and small: exclude the store when staging in build_user_commit, e.g. `run(&["add", "-A", "--", ".", ":(exclude).asp"], false)` (pathspec magic works with GIT_INDEX_FILE temp index), or write `/.asp/` into the fork's .git/info/exclude before staging; plus extend the promote test with `assert!(!tree.contains(".asp"))`. Severity critical stands: this is the product's single landing path, it breaks the advertised merge flow unconditionally, bloats user repos, and silently exfiltrates never-committed local file history if the branch is pushed — release-blocking for v0.1.0.

confirmed.title: kill -9 during checkpoint or restore can turn big files into 104-byte pointer-JSON files that the engine then legitimizes; doctor sees nothing
confirmed.description: Two crash windows around the large-blob manifest violate 'kill -9 safe at any line': (A) checkpoint_locked writes the checkpoint ref (workspace.rs:364-365) BEFORE the meta/manifest ref (workspace.rs:386-388). A kill between them yields a resolvable checkpoint whose tree contains pointer blobs but has no manifest. A later full restore to it runs checkout-index (writes the pointer JSON over the real file), finds manifest_for() == None, takes the 'target predates big files' branch and CLEARS bigfiles.json — the user's 2MB file is now a 104-byte `{"asp_ptr":...}` file on disk and the engine has forgotten it was ever managed. (B) within full restore itself, checkout-index (line 734) writes pointer JSON over every big file before the CAS materialization loop (lines 772-793) replaces them; a kill in between leaves pointer files on disk. In both cases the next hook-driven checkpoint captures the corrupted state (today as a deletion, per the shrink bug; as pointer-JSON content once that is fixed), and `asp doctor` reports 'workspace is healthy'. The bytes still exist in .asp/blobs but nothing surfaces that. Also: manifest_for maps ALL git errors to Ok(None) (workspace.rs:629), so a transient git failure during restore triggers the same wrong branch. The torture suite's restore test uses only small files, so this is uncovered.
confirmed.evidence:
Repro A: checkpoint a 2MB model.bin (threshold 1MB); `git update-ref -d refs/asp/meta/1` (simulating the kill); `asp restore 1` -> model.bin on disk is 104 bytes: {"asp_ptr":1,"blake3":"adc3...","size":2097152}; bigfiles.json files:{}; `asp doctor` -> 'workspace is healthy'.
Repro B: write the pointer JSON at model.bin (simulating kill between checkout-index and the CAS loop); next `asp checkpoint` records the path as deleted; doctor healthy.
confirmed.suggested_fix: (1) Write the meta ref BEFORE the checkpoint ref — an orphan meta ref is harmless. (2) Make restore independent of the manifest as a backstop: after checkout-index, scan written files with blobs::parse_pointer and materialize any pointer from CAS (the pointer itself names the hash); error with a hint if the CAS blob is missing instead of silently keeping the pointer file. (3) Teach scan_changes/doctor to recognize on-disk files whose content parses as an asp pointer whose hash exists in CAS — crashed-restore residue — and re-materialize rather than capture. (4) Add big files to the kill-9 restore torture test.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: Confirmed by direct reproduction against target/release/asp, not just code reading. Repro A: with blob_threshold_mb=1, checkpointed a 2MB model.bin, deleted refs/asp/meta/1 (the exact state a kill -9 between workspace.rs:365 and :388 leaves — two separate git subprocess spawns, so the window is real), then `asp restore 1`: model.bin became a 104-byte {"asp_ptr":...} JSON file, bigfiles.json was cleared via the 'target predates big files' branch (workspace.rs:794-803), and the restore's own auto-checkpoint #3 captured the pointer JSON as the file's legitimate content (no meta ref). `asp doctor` reported 'workspace is healthy'. Repro B: reproduced restore's intermediate state (read-tree + checkout-index against .asp/shadow.index, CAS loop at :772-793 not yet run); the next `asp checkpoint` captured model.bin as a regular 104-byte blob whose oid is the cached pointer_oid, emptied bigfiles.json, and doctor stayed healthy. (Minor wording correction: repro B captures pointer-JSON content, not a deletion.) Also confirmed: manifest_for maps ALL git errors to Ok(None) at workspace.rs:629; doctor's only big-file check (workspace.rs:1438-1466) reads the very bigfiles.json that both failure modes empty; the torture suite's write_files uses only tiny files (torture.rs:60-67) so the big-file path has zero kill -9 coverage. This directly violates trust-model claims (2) 'kill -9 safe at any line' and (4) errors carry hints. Severity stays major rather than critical because bytes are not unrecoverably lost: the CAS still holds the content and the pre-restore safety checkpoint has a valid manifest — `asp restore 2` recovered the real 2MB file — but the corruption is silent, gets legitimized by subsequent checkpoints, and doctor blesses it, so the user has no signal. Caveat on the suggested fix: merely writing the meta ref first creates a new hazard — next_seq() (workspace.rs:642-646) ignores meta refs, so an orphaned meta/N would attach to the NEXT checkpoint N and mis-materialize on restore if that checkpoint has no big files; the manifest-independent restore backstop (parse pointer files after checkout-index, materialize from the hash named in the pointer itself) plus doctor/scan awareness of pointer residue is the robust remedy, and the torture suite needs big files added.

confirmed.title: No-op fast path returns the wrong tree after a restore whose only delta is big-file content: post-restore checkpoint skipped, diff and checkpoint lie
confirmed.description: After a full restore where the only difference between the safety state and the target is a big file's content (cp1: model.bin v1 -> cp2: v2 -> restore 1), the staged-deletion filter at workspace.rs:556-562 swallows the only porcelain evidence (the 'D ' entry for the pointer path is assumed to be asp bookkeeping, but here HEAD=cp2's pointer differs from the restored v1), and the big-file stat-check matches the just-materialized file. `changed` ends up empty, so stage_tree's fast path returns tree_of(HEAD) = cp2's tree (v2 pointer) — not the actual working state (v1). Results, all verified: the restore's post-checkpoint is skipped (RestoreReport.safety_seq and post both null); `asp checkpoint` immediately after the restore says 'nothing changed since the last checkpoint' although disk == cp1 != cp2; `asp diff 2` (cp2 vs working tree) returns ZERO rows although the working tree is v1; `asp diff 1` reports model.bin Modified although the working tree exactly equals cp1. Downstream, `asp fork` taken in this state registers fork_point_seq=2 (claiming v2) while the fork physically carries v1, and discard's unpromoted-work guard compares against the same wrong tree. The timeline self-corrects only at the next checkpoint that touches some other file.
confirmed.evidence: Repro (threshold 1MB): cp1 = {s.txt, model.bin v1}; rewrite model.bin to v2; cp2; `asp restore 1` -> disk sha256 == v1 (restore correct), but `asp log` shows no post-restore checkpoint; `asp --json diff 2` -> rows: []; `asp --json diff 1` -> model.bin status M; `asp checkpoint` -> "nothing changed since the last checkpoint".
confirmed.suggested_fix: The fast path's premise ('changed empty => head tree describes the working state') is false whenever the current bigfiles set/hashes differ from HEAD's manifest. Before taking the fast path, compare bigfiles against the manifest of HEAD's seq (or compare each bf entry's cached pointer_oid against the blob at that path in HEAD's tree); fall through to real staging on mismatch. Add an engine test: checkpoint, change only the big file, checkpoint, restore the first, assert a post-restore checkpoint exists and `diff <first>` is empty.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning:
CONFIRMED by live repro against target/release/asp (threshold 1MB, /tmp/asp-repro). Sequence: cp1 = {s.txt, model.bin v1 (2MB 'A')}; rewrite model.bin to v2 (2MB 'B'); cp2; `asp restore 1`. Verified results: (1) disk sha256 of model.bin == v1 after restore (restore itself is correct); (2) restore's journal entry has "safety_seq": null AND "post_seq": null — the post-restore checkpoint was silently skipped, so `asp log` shows HEAD still at cp2 while disk is cp1; (3) `asp --json diff 2` returns rows: [] (claims working tree == cp2 — false); (4) `asp --json diff 1` reports model.bin status "M" (claims working tree differs from cp1 — false, it is byte-identical); (5) `asp checkpoint` says "nothing changed since the last checkpoint" and refuses to capture the actual v1 state; (6) `asp fork` in this state registers fork_point_seq=2 in .asp/forks.json while the fork physically carries v1 (sha verified); (7) self-correction confirmed: touching s.txt and checkpointing produced cp3 with 2 files changed (the slow staging path spliced the v1 pointer), after which diff is truthful again.

Root cause verified in code, exactly as claimed: crates/asp-core/src/workspace.rs:556-561 — scan_changes swallows the porcelain 'D ' entry for the pointer path (assumed to be asp's own index bookkeeping) whenever bigfiles.json contains the path and the file exists on disk; but after a full restore, restore() at workspace.rs:772-793 rewrites bigfiles.json with the target's (v1) blake3/size and the mtime of the just-materialized file, so the stat-check at workspace.rs:583-601 also sees no change. `changed` ends up empty and the fast path at workspace.rs:443-451 returns tree_of(HEAD) — cp2's tree with the v2 pointer — as "the working state". The fast path's comment ("the head tree already describes the working state exactly") is false whenever the bigfiles cache disagrees with HEAD's pointer manifest, which is precisely the post-restore condition when the only delta is big-file content.

Severity: major is correct. No permanent byte loss was demonstrated (v1 == cp1 remains in the store; any subsequent edit to the big file defeats the fast path via the mtime/size stat-check, and the timeline self-corrects at the next real checkpoint). But it is a genuine data-safety/trust-model defect for v0.1.0: after an ordinary restore, the core read model lies in both directions (diff, checkpoint no-op detection, log position) and fork provenance metadata is wrong (fork_point_seq=2 carrying v1 content), so an agent or user acting on `asp diff`/`asp checkpoint` output makes decisions from false state. The suggested fix is sound: before taking the fast path, verify each bigfiles entry's pointer against the blob at that path in HEAD's tree (or compare against HEAD's seq manifest) and fall through to real staging on mismatch; the slow path already handles this correctly (demonstrated by cp3 capturing 2 files). Add the proposed engine test: checkpoint, change only the big file, checkpoint, restore the first, assert post-restore checkpoint exists and `diff <first>` is empty.

confirmed.title: status() is blind to big-file edits — reports clean, and undo then restores the wrong checkpoint (two states back)
confirmed.description: Managed big files are excluded from the shadow index by design, so `git status` can never see their changes; only scan_changes stat-checks them (workspace.rs:583-602). status() never consults that logic, so editing only a 100MB managed file yields dirty=0/untracked=0/deleted=0 — `asp status` tells the user nothing changed. undo() uses this status to pick its branch (workspace.rs:881-884): with a big-file-only edit it wrongly takes the 'clean' branch and restores to the checkpoint BEFORE last, instead of reverting the edit to the last checkpoint. Verified: cp1 = 2MB of 'A', cp2 = 'B', edit to 'C', `asp undo` -> disk is 'A' (cp1), not 'B' (cp2). The 'C' edit is preserved in the auto safety checkpoint (restore's internal scan does see big files), so nothing is unrecoverable, but the user silently lands two states back and the v2 state is evicted from the working tree contrary to the documented dirty-undo semantics.
confirmed.evidence: Repro: threshold 1MB; cp1 model.bin='A'x2M; cp2 ='B'x2M; edit to 'C'x2M; `asp --json status` -> dirty_files:0, untracked_files:0, deleted_files:0; `asp undo` -> head -c1 model.bin == 'A' (expected 'B' per 'if dirty, revert to the last checkpoint').
confirmed.suggested_fix: In status(), after the porcelain scan, stat-check bigfiles.json entries exactly like scan_changes does (size/mtime mismatch -> count as dirty; missing -> deleted; new >=threshold untracked files -> untracked). undo() then branches correctly for free. Test: big-file-only edit must produce a dirty status and dirty-undo must return to the LAST checkpoint.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: Fully reproduced with the release binary at /tmp/asp-bigfile-repro (threshold 1MB): cp1 model.bin='A'x2M, cp2='B'x2M, edit to 'C'x2M; `asp --json status` returned dirty_files:0/untracked_files:0/deleted_files:0, and `asp undo` restored target_seq:1 ('A') instead of cp2 ('B'). Code confirms the mechanism: status() (crates/asp-core/src/workspace.rs:271-320) only parses `git status --porcelain -z` and filters the expected staged-deletion entries for managed big files; the files themselves are invisible to git status because stage_tree() puts them in the shadow excludes (436-441) and removes their pointer paths from the index after write-tree (509-518). Only scan_changes() (565-602) stat-checks bigfiles.json, and status() never calls it. undo() (875-896) branches on status() at 881-884, so a big-file-only edit takes the 'clean' branch and lands two checkpoints back, violating the documented 'revert uncommitted changes if dirty' contract. The edit is preserved in the auto safety checkpoint (verified: restore 3 yields 'C', restore 2 yields 'B'), so nothing is permanently unrecoverable — hence major, not critical. It matters for v0.1.0: with the default 50MB threshold this is exactly the model-weights/dataset workflow the CAS sidecar is built for, and it makes both `asp status` (reports clean on a dirty tree) and `asp undo` (silently wrong target) lie. The suggested fix — read-only stat-check of bigfiles.json entries inside status(), mirroring scan_changes — is correct and fixes undo's branch for free.

confirmed.title: Repeated `asp undo` ping-pongs between the last two states; it can never reach older checkpoints
confirmed.description: Every clean undo restores refs.latest-1 and then appends a post-restore checkpoint of that older tree as the NEW latest seq. The next clean undo computes prev = the seq just below latest — which is the state undo just left. Verified: states v1,v2,v3 at cp1..3; undo -> v2, undo -> v3, undo -> v2, undo -> v3, forever; v1 is unreachable via undo. For an agent told to 'undo until X' (agents are first-class users per the brief) this is an infinite loop; for a human, 'step back one checkpoint' (the CLI's own description: 'step back / revert agent damage') silently stops working after one step. The workaround (`asp restore #seq`) exists but nothing points the user at it. The engine test undo_dirty_then_clean only exercises two undos and so never observes the ping-pong.
confirmed.evidence: Repro: cp1=v1, cp2=v2, cp3=v3, clean; four consecutive `asp undo` produce: v2, v3, v2, v3. Cause: workspace.rs:886-894 picks `refs.range(..latest).next_back()` where latest is always the post-restore checkpoint created by the previous undo (workspace.rs:847-851).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Track an undo cursor: when the tree is clean and the latest checkpoint is the post-checkpoint of a Restore journal entry (detail.post_seq == latest), continue the streak from that restore's target_seq instead — i.e. undo to the checkpoint preceding target_seq. The journal already records target_seq/post_seq (workspace.rs:855-861), so this needs no format change. Add a test walking back three states via three undos.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: Reproduced end-to-end with the release binary: in /tmp/undo-pingpong with checkpoints v1/v2/v3, six consecutive clean `asp undo` calls yield v2,v3,v2,v3,v2,v3 — v1 is never reachable via undo. Code confirms the mechanism: undo (crates/asp-core/src/workspace.rs:874-897) picks refs.range(..latest).next_back(), while restore (workspace.rs:843-851) always appends a post-restore checkpoint of the older tree as the new latest (checkpoint_locked at workspace.rs:349-354 dedups only against HEAD's tree, so the post-checkpoint is always created). The next clean undo therefore targets the state just left. The only test of consecutive undos (crates/asp-core/tests/engine.rs:148 undo_dirty_then_clean) does one clean + one dirty undo and never observes the loop. The MCP description (crates/asp/src/mcp.rs:143-147) tells agents undo "goes back one checkpoint" when clean, so an agent doing 'undo until X' infinite-loops; no hint points at `asp restore #seq`. The suggested fix is feasible without a format change — journal restore entries already carry target_seq and post_seq (verified in .asp/journal.jsonl). Severity: confirmed as major, though it is a broken-semantics/agent-loop bug rather than data loss — all checkpoints remain intact and reachable via `asp restore #N`, so nothing is destroyed; but a headline command ('step back / revert agent damage') silently stops progressing after one step, which matters for v0.1.0.

confirmed.title: Arbitrary file write outside workspace via restore CAS materialization (malicious .asp store, RCE)
confirmed.description: restore/undo writes big-file pointers via root.join(ptr.path) (:778, :824) then restore_from_cas (blobs.rs:117-133); no path containment and the blob is not hash-verified; an attacker .asp store (manifest+blobs) with a .. or symlinked-parent path writes anywhere the user can -> RCE. asp undo hits the same path.
confirmed.evidence: PoC on release binary: manifest pointer ../../../../tmp/X/escaped.txt at refs/asp/meta/1 plus a planted .asp/blobs entry; asp restore 1 wrote escaped.txt OUTSIDE the workspace.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Reject pointer paths that are absolute, contain .., or whose canonical parent is outside root; refuse symlinked components; verify the blob hash. Same at :824.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning:
CONFIRMED on the shipping release binary (target/release/asp). The reviewer's claim reproduces exactly, and is actually worse than described.

Root cause: restore/undo materializes big-file pointers by joining an attacker-controlled manifest path onto the workspace root with no containment and no hash verification:
- crates/asp-core/src/workspace.rs:778 `let abs = self.layout.root.join(&ptr.path);` then :779 `blobs::restore_from_cas(&self.layout.blobs(), &ptr.blake3, &abs)?` — the full-tree restore path used by the default `asp restore <seq>`.
- crates/asp-core/src/workspace.rs:824-825 — the targeted-restore path (same flaw; lower reach since the malicious path must be passed as an arg).
- crates/asp/.../undo at workspace.rs:884 and :894 calls `self.restore(&..., &[], source)` with empty paths, so `asp undo` routes through the same full-tree materialization at :778.
- crates/asp-core/src/blobs.rs:117-133 `restore_from_cas` actively creates parent dirs (:129-131), removes any existing dst (:126-128), then clonefiles (:132). It looks the blob up purely by filename (`cas_dir.join(hash)`) and never verifies BLAKE3, so the attacker fully controls written content; `clone_file`/clonefile also preserves the source mode (executable bit).
- The manifest is read at workspace.rs:615-631 (`manifest_for`) straight from `refs/asp/meta/<seq>` with serde and zero path sanitization. The grep for is_absolute/.. /canonical/components turned up nothing on this path.

Reproduced three escalations:
1) `..` traversal: manifest path `../victim_dir/PWNED_via_restore.txt` -> a 2MB attacker blob written to `/tmp/asp_poc/victim_dir/`, OUTSIDE the workspace root `/tmp/asp_poc/ws`. (A trailing `update-index` at :781 errors AFTER the write — `restore_from_cas` at :779 has already completed, so the error does not prevent the escape.)
2) Absolute path: manifest path `/tmp/asp_poc/victim_existing.txt` -> PathBuf::join replaces the base, OVERWRITING an existing victim file (27-byte original clobbered by the 2MB attacker blob) = arbitrary file overwrite / data loss anywhere the user can write.
3) Clean RCE + sacred-.git violation: a PLAIN in-tree path `.git/hooks/post-checkout` (no `..` needed, so git's own path checks never apply) wrote an executable hook (-rwxr-xr-x, exec bit preserved via clonefile) into the user's REAL `.git`. The next `git checkout`/`pull` runs attacker code. This directly breaks the "user .git is sacred" non-negotiable pillar; an absolute path to `~/.zshrc` is an equivalent RCE.

Threat model relevance for v0.1.0: exploitation requires the victim to run a recovery command (`asp restore`/`asp undo`) on an attacker-supplied `.asp` store (a checkpoint ref + a meta manifest + a `.asp/blobs/<hash>` payload, all attacker-controlled). That is squarely the product's use case: workspaces over real directories that are branchable/forkable and "content-addressed and sync-ready from day one" (docs/design/format.md:3,30; v1-brief.md:51). Even before sync ships, "git clone a repo that ships a `.asp/` dir, then run asp" is a realistic OSS-launch vector. The victim only runs a benign recovery command yet gets arbitrary file write/overwrite and RCE — a real trust-boundary defect, not a hypothetical.

Suggested fix (apply at both :778 and :824, before calling restore_from_cas): reject `ptr.path` if it is absolute, contains any `..`/non-Normal component, or whose lexically-resolved location is outside `self.layout.root`; refuse any path whose existing parent components are symlinks (defeats symlinked-parent escapes); and additionally refuse in-tree writes into `.git/` to honor the sacred-.git pillar. In blobs.rs `restore_from_cas`, verify the materialized blob's BLAKE3 equals the requested `hash` (defense-in-depth; also catches a corrupt/poisoned CAS). A torture/property test for traversal-bearing manifests should accompany the fix.

confirmed.title: promote commits the whole .asp/ sidecar (shadow store + journal) into the promoted branch
confirmed.description: build_user_commit runs git add -A . (:1566); nothing excludes .asp/ and git does not auto-ignore the bare .asp/shadow.git, so the whole shadow store + journal.jsonl land in every promoted branch, leaking full checkpoint history and session provenance into a shared/PR branch.
confirmed.evidence: PoC: git ls-tree -r of the promoted branch lists .asp/journal.jsonl and .asp/shadow.git/objects/... (whole store); gitignored .env was correctly excluded.

confirmed.verifier_reasoning: Reproduced end-to-end with target/release/asp in /tmp: after init/checkpoint/fork/promote, `git ls-tree -r asp/attempt-1` lists 32 .asp/ paths — journal.jsonl, workspace.json, config.toml, shadow.index, and the entire bare shadow.git (objects/refs/logs) — while gitignored .env was correctly excluded, confirming the mechanism. Two qualifications to the claim: (1) shadow checkpoints respect the user's .gitignore, so gitignored secrets are NOT embedded in the leaked shadow objects — the leak is checkpoint history of non-ignored files plus journal/session provenance, not credentials by default; (2) impact is worse than a leak alone: the tool's own printed hint `git merge asp/attempt-1` fails immediately with "untracked working tree files would be overwritten by merge" on all .asp paths, so the promote→merge happy path is broken for every user out of the box. confirmed.title: promote commits the fork's entire .asp/ store into the user's branch; the suggested `git merge` then fails
confirmed.description: build_user_commit() runs `git add -A .` in the fork with a temp index. `.asp/` is untracked and not gitignored (asp init neither adds it to .gitignore nor suggests it), so the promoted commit contains the fork's whole .asp store: shadow.git objects/refs/hooks samples, journal.jsonl, lock, shadow.index, config.toml, forks.json, race.log for race lanes. The flagship fork→promote→merge loop fails out of the box on every repo that has not manually gitignored .asp (the default state — `git status` shows `?? .asp/` right after init).

':(exclude).asp'` (and add an assertion to the existing promote_lands_branch_in_user_repo test that the promoted tree contains no .asp entry). (3) The success message printed by crates/asp/src/main.rs:543-546 ('merge it: git merge asp/win') then fails exactly as claimed: 'error: The following untracked working tree files would be overwritten by merge: .asp/config.toml ... Please move or remove them before you merge.' — git's own advice coaches the user to delete the live checkpoint store. (4) Boundary check: with `.asp/` in .gitignore the whole loop works cleanly (diff = a.txt only, merge succeeds), proving the broken case is precisely the default state and that the proposed `:(exclude).asp` pathspec fix is the right shape. confirmed.title: Repeated `asp undo` oscillates between the last two states and can never reach older checkpoints
confirmed.description: undo() on a clean tree restores to 'the checkpoint before the latest', but every restore itself appends new auto-checkpoints ('auto: before/after restore to #N'), so the second undo's target is the state you just left. With a plain linear history v1→v2→v3: undo → v2 (correct), undo again → v3 (forward!), undo again → v2, forever. You can never reach v1 via undo; `asp restore 1` is the only way, and nothing tells you that. Worse, after undoing agent damage, a second reflexive undo silently re-applies the damage (it re-deleted a file during my own testing). The help text 'Step back one checkpoint' (main.rs:75) and the README pitch 'asp undo — step back, including bash side-effects' both imply walking backward through history. This is the hero recovery feature behaving as an undo/redo toggle without ever saying so.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced: 3 checkpoints v1/v2/v3 of f.txt, then `asp undo` three times → f.txt contents: v2, v3, v2. Cause: undo picks `refs.range(..latest).next_back()` where `latest` is the auto 'state after restore' checkpoint created by the previous undo, whose predecessor is the pre-restore state.
confirmed.suggested_fix: When choosing the undo target, skip the bookkeeping checkpoints created by restore (walk past 'auto: before/after restore' entries to the checkpoint preceding the restore chain), giving repeated undo true walk-back semantics; or keep toggle semantics but add an explicit `asp redo`, print 'undo again returns to #N (redo)' after each undo, and fix the help text and README to say so.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: Fully reproduced with the release binary. (1) Oscillation: created checkpoints v1/v2/v3 of f.txt in /tmp, ran `asp undo` four times → f.txt contents v2, v3, v2, v3; v1 is permanently unreachable via undo. `asp log` confirms the cause: each undo's restore appends an 'auto: state after restore to #N' checkpoint (workspace.rs:844-851), and undo() picks the predecessor of the latest ref (workspace.rs:875-895, `refs.range(..latest).next_back()`), which is always the state just left — a 2-state toggle, despite the code comment claiming 'undo-stack semantics'. (2) Damage re-application: checkpointed precious.txt, deleted it, first undo restored it, second undo silently deleted it again ('0 files written, 1 removed') with no warning that it moved forward. (3) Misleading docs confirmed: main.rs:75 'Step back one checkpoint', README.md:40 'step back, including bash side-effects', and mcp.rs:144-146 tells agents 'if the tree is clean, go back one checkpoint' — so agents looping workspace_undo oscillate too. Only one clean-undo invocation is tested (engine.rs:148); repeated undo is untested and toggle semantics are documented nowhere. Not critical because no data is lost — every state remains a checkpoint and `asp restore N` recovers anything (the dirty path even prints a recovery hint) — but it is a genuine behavioral bug in the hero recovery feature that silently re-applies undone damage and contradicts all user-facing documentation. Major is the right severity for v0.1.0 launch.

confirmed.title: Gitignored files (.env — the README's own example) are silently unprotected by checkpoint/undo
confirmed.description: The shadow-git capture respects the user's .gitignore, so anything gitignored — most commonly .env — is never checkpointed and `asp undo` will not restore it. The README's opening pitch names exactly this case: 'they run bash, touch .env ... The state your agents actually produce has no safety net. asp is that safety net' (line 16), and the quickstart comment says 'capture everything, untracked files included' (line 33). docs/why-not-git-worktrees.md section 2 says 'asp checkpoints capture the full source tree — untracked files included — so asp undo reverts bash damage'. Only the comparison table's fine print ('respects .gitignore + derived-state excludes') hints at the truth. There is also no config escape hatch: config.toml supports only excludes/extra_excludes (crates/asp-core/src/config.rs:33-44), no force-include. A user whose agent destroys a gitignored .env discovers the safety net has a hole exactly where the marketing said it didn't.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced: repo with `.env` in .gitignore, `echo SECRET=original > .env`, `asp checkpoint` (captured 2 files, not .env), overwrite .env, `asp undo` → .env still contains SECRET=DESTROYED while tracked files were restored. No warning at checkpoint time that ignored files were skipped.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Pick one: (a) add `capture.includes` (gitignore-negation lines written to shadow info/exclude) and document protecting .env as the example; or at minimum (b) fix the copy — README line 16/33 and why-not-git-worktrees.md §2 must say 'untracked-but-not-gitignored files', add an FAQ entry ('Is my .env checkpointed? Not if it is gitignored — forks carry it, checkpoints do not'), and have `asp init` or first checkpoint print 'N gitignored files will not be checkpointed (see capture settings)'.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: CONFIRMED by full end-to-end reproduction with the release binary, plus code inspection. (1) Reproduction in /tmp: git repo with `.env` in .gitignore containing SECRET=original; `asp init && asp checkpoint -m baseline` reported "checkpoint #1 — 2 file(s)" (.gitignore + main.rs, not .env), with no warning that a gitignored file was skipped. Overwrote `.env` with SECRET=DESTROYED and main.rs with garbage; `asp checkpoint` reported "1 file(s)" (the .env change was invisible); `asp undo` reported "restored to checkpoint #1 (2 files written, 0 removed)" — main.rs was restored, but `.env` still contained SECRET=DESTROYED. (2) Mechanism in code: the shadow repo runs `git add -A` / status with GIT_WORK_TREE set to the user's directory (crates/asp-core/src/gitx.rs:33-49, 173-176; workspace.rs:434-475), so git reads the worktree's `.gitignore` and silently excludes anything it matches. (3) No escape hatch confirmed: config.rs:31-44 offers only `excludes`/`extra_excludes` (with serde deny_unknown_fields, so no hidden option). I tested `extra_excludes = ["!.env"]` — checkpoint still said "nothing changed" after modifying .env, because patterns written to the shadow repo's info/exclude (gitx.rs:164-170) have LOWER precedence than the worktree's .gitignore in git's exclusion ordering, so even a negation cannot force-include. (4) The docs claims are exactly as described: README.md:16 leads with "They run bash, touch `.env` ... The state your agents actually produce has no safety net. `asp` is that safety net"; README.md:33 quickstart comment says "capture everything, untracked files included"; docs/why-not-git-worktrees.md:13 says "asp checkpoints capture the full source tree — untracked files included — ... so `asp undo` reverts bash damage". The only accurate disclosures are fine print: the README comparison table line 55 and docs/design/format.md:26 ("respects `.gitignore` + derived-state excludes"). One fairness note: forks DO carry .env (verified — the clonefile fork contained the live .env), so v1-brief.md:27's fork claim is accurate; but the checkpoint/undo flow — the one the README's safety-net pitch and quickstart center on — demonstrably does not protect the README's own headline example, silently. The behavior is by design per format.md, so this is a docs/UX mismatch rather than a code bug, but for a product whose stated trust model is "the safety net" it is launch-relevant: a user loses gitignored data they were explicitly told was captured, with no checkpoint-time warning and no configuration workaround. Suggested fix as filed stands: either add a force-include mechanism (note: it must be implemented via pathspec or explicit `git add -f`, NOT info/exclude negation, since info/exclude cannot override .gitignore) or fix README:16/33 and why-not-git-worktrees.md:13 to say "untracked-but-not-gitignored", plus a first-checkpoint notice when gitignored files exist. Severity: major (silent gap in the advertised safety net on its own marquee example), not critical (no corruption of existing data; behavior is documented in fine print; forks do cover it).

confirmed.description: build_user_commit() stages the fork's whole tree with `git add -A .` against the user's gitignore only. Nothing (init, fork, promote, README) ever excludes `.asp/` from the user repo, so every promoted branch contains the full checkpoint store: shadow.git objects, shadow.index, journal.jsonl, the lock file, and — on real workspaces — the CAS blobs in .asp/blobs (50MB+ each). Add an assertion to promote_lands_branch_in_user_repo that `ls-tree` contains no path starting with .asp/. Optionally also have `asp init` print a one-line suggestion to gitignore `.asp/` when a user .git exists — but the promote-side exclusion must not depend on it.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: CONFIRMED by direct reproduction with target/release/asp. (1) Code: /Users/apple/Projects/agentspaces/crates/asp-core/src/workspace.rs:1566 — build_user_commit() runs `run(&["add", "-A", "."], false)?` in the fork dir with only GIT_INDEX_FILE overridden; the only `.asp/` exclusion in the codebase is shadow_excludes() in crates/asp-core/src/config.rs:101, which applies only to the shadow repo's info/exclude, never to the user-repo commit. clone_tree (crates/asp-core/src/fork.rs:26) copies the whole tree, so every fork contains a live `.asp/` store. (2) Reproduction: git init + commit, asp init/checkpoint/fork --name winner, edit in fork, asp promote winner → `git ls-tree -r --name-only asp/winner` lists 31 .asp/ paths (.asp/journal.jsonl, .asp/lock, .asp/shadow.git/objects/*, .asp/shadow.index, .asp/workspace.json, ...) alongside app.txt. (3) The CLI itself then prints "merge it: git merge asp/winner" (crates/asp/src/main.rs ~543-546), and running exactly that fails: "error: The following untracked working tree files would be overwritten by merge: .asp/config.toml ... Aborting" (exit 1). (4) No mitigation exists: neither asp init, README.md, nor docs ever instruct users to gitignore .asp/ — README even claims "your .git is never touched" and design notes claim ".gitignore-safe". If a user works around the merge error by removing the live .asp dir, the store lands in their mainline history, which is worse. The suggested fix (`git add -A -- . ':(exclude).asp'` plus a test assertion that ls-tree contains no .asp/ paths) is correct and minimal. Release-blocking for v0.1.0; severity critical stands.

confirmed.title: asp doctor --fix recursively deletes any sibling directory matching <dir>@<name> that it misjudges as a torn fork — including user-made cp -r backups with new work
confirmed.description: The torn-fork detector treats a sibling dir named `<dirname>@<anything>` with an .asp dir as torn when (a) workspace.json fails to read for ANY reason (read_json(...).ok() == None covers permission errors and transient IO, not just missing files), or (b) its workspace id equals the parent's. A manual `cp -r proj proj@backup` (the @-suffix is asp's own visible naming convention, so users will mimic it) carries the same workspace id, and doctor --fix then remove_dir_all's it — including any work added to the copy after the backup. The CLI labels --fix "apply safe repairs". For a tool whose brief says "any data-loss incident is fatal", an irreversible recursive delete behind a heuristic is too aggressive.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced: asp init + checkpoint in /tmp/asp-exp4/proj; `cp -r proj proj@backup`; `echo precious > proj@backup/precious.txt`; `asp doctor --fix` → "warning: torn fork clone at /private/tmp/asp-exp4/proj@backup (fork creation crashed mid-flight) [fixed]" and the directory, including precious.txt, is gone. Code: `let torn = match identity { None => true, Some(m) => m.id == self.meta.id };` then `if fix { std::fs::remove_dir_all(entry.path())?; }` (workspace.rs:1402-1411).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Never remove_dir_all from doctor --fix. Quarantine instead: rename to `<dir>.torn-<timestamp>` (atomic, reversible) and tell the user to delete it after inspection, or require an explicit `asp doctor --remove-torn <path>`. Additionally, only classify as torn when workspace.json is genuinely absent (distinguish NotFound from other read errors), and skip auto-removal entirely if the candidate's tree diverges from the parent's fork-point checkpoint (divergence implies someone worked in it).
confirmed.verifier_reasoning:
CONFIRMED — reproduced twice with target/release/asp, and the second reproduction is strictly worse than the original claim.

Code evidence (/Users/apple/Projects/agentspaces/crates/asp-core/src/workspace.rs:1400-1415): `let identity: Option<WorkspaceMeta> = read_json(&candidate.workspace_json()).ok();` collapses ALL read errors (NotFound, EACCES, transient IO, corrupt JSON) into None; `let torn = match identity { None => true, Some(m) => m.id == self.meta.id };` then `if fix { std::fs::remove_dir_all(entry.path())?; }`. The `registered` flag computed at line 1408 from the fork registry is NOT consulted in the torn branch — it only gates the Info message in the else branch (lines 1424-1433).

Repro 1 (the claimed scenario): `asp init` + checkpoint in /tmp/.../proj; `cp -r proj proj@backup`; `echo "precious work" > proj@backup/precious.txt`; `asp doctor --fix` from proj → "warning: torn fork clone at .../proj@backup (fork creation crashed mid-flight) [fixed]" and the entire backup including precious.txt is irreversibly gone. The cp -r copy carries the parent's workspace id with no parent link, so `m.id == self.meta.id` classifies it torn. The `@` naming is asp's own user-visible convention (`asp fork --name mybranch` prints "fork mybranch → .../proj@mybranch"), so users mimicking it for backups is plausible.

Repro 2 (worse, from the claim's read-error sub-point): created a LEGITIMATE fork via `asp fork --name mybranch` (completed fixup: new id + parent link, registered in forks.json, doctor reports healthy). Then `chmod 000 proj@mybranch/.asp/workspace.json` (simulating a transient permission/IO error) and ran `asp doctor --fix` → asp recursively deleted its OWN registered, active, completed fork including new work added to it ("registered fork DELETED"). The registry knew the path was a real fork and the code ignored that knowledge.

Severity context: the command is documented as "apply safe repairs" (crates/asp/src/main.rs:130-132, 659-660), and the product brief states "any data-loss incident is fatal" and "data loss is a one-strike kill for a storage tool" (docs/design/v1-brief.md:56,76). An in-product command performing irreversible remove_dir_all behind a heuristic that misfires on both user backups and asp's own registered forks is a release-blocking data-loss bug, not a code-quality nit — I adjust severity from major to critical.

Suggested fix (concrete): (1) never remove_dir_all in doctor --fix — quarantine by renaming to `<dir>.torn-<unix-ts>` (atomic rename on same filesystem, reversible) and print a hint to inspect/delete manually, or require explicit `asp doctor --remove-torn <path>`; (2) classify as torn only when workspace.json read fails with io::ErrorKind::NotFound — propagate or warn-without-fix on EACCES/other errors; (3) skip any destructive action when `registered` is true (line 1408 already computes this — use it in the torn branch); (4) optionally, before quarantining, check the candidate tree against the fork-point checkpoint and refuse if it diverges (divergence implies real work). A torture-suite case for "doctor --fix must never delete a registered fork or a same-id sibling containing files newer than the fork point" would lock this in.

confirmed.title: Non-UTF-8 filenames (legal on Linux) permanently break checkpointing, and the hook path makes the failure silent
confirmed.description: scan_changes() converts paths from `git status --porcelain -z` with String::from_utf8_lossy (workspace.rs:546, also status() at :288 and the restore deletion loop at :753-756), replacing invalid bytes with U+FFFD. The corrupted path is then fed to `git --literal-pathspecs add -A --pathspec-from-file=- --pathspec-file-nul` (workspace.rs:464-473), which exits 128 with "pathspec ... did not match any files" because no such file exists. macOS APFS rejects non-UTF-8 names at the FS layer, but Linux ext4/btrfs/XFS (launch platforms) permit them — one stray latin-1 filename from an agent's bash command or an extracted tarball makes EVERY subsequent checkpoint fail until the file is renamed (the workspace has >0 changes so the no-op fast path never engages, and below the 2000-file threshold the broken pathspec path is always taken). Through the hooks (`asp hook-event` swallows all errors and exits 0, hooks.rs:27-31), auto-checkpointing silently stops while the agent keeps making changes — the undo guarantee is silently off. Same root cause makes full restore silently skip deleting such files. The generic error hint ("run `asp doctor`") does not name the offending file, and doctor has no check for it.
confirmed.evidence: Simulated the exact failure: in an asp workspace, `printf 'caf\xef\xbf\xbd.txt' | GIT_DIR=.asp/shadow.git GIT_WORK_TREE=. GIT_INDEX_FILE=.asp/shadow.index git --literal-pathspecs add -A --pathspec-from-file=- --pathspec-file-nul` → "fatal: pathspec 'caf\u{fffd}.txt' did not match any files", exit 128 — which gitx::run_with_stdin turns into GitFailed and checkpoint() propagates. Property tests only generate `[a-zA-Z0-9]` names (crates/asp-core/tests/properties.rs:85) so this is untested. APFS check: creating b'caf\xe9.txt' on macOS fails with EILSEQ, confirming Linux-only exposure.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Keep paths as raw bytes end to end: parse `status -z` records into Vec<u8>/OsString and write the raw bytes (NUL-separated) straight into the --pathspec-from-file stdin instead of round-tripping through from_utf8_lossy; use OsStr for filesystem operations in restore. If full byte-cleanliness is too big for v0.1.0, at minimum detect a lossy conversion (path contains U+FFFD after conversion) and fail with a corrective hint naming the file ("rename <file>: asp requires UTF-8 filenames in v1"), and add a doctor check — never let hooks silently stop checkpointing.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning:
CONFIRMED by full end-to-end reproduction on Linux with the real binary. I built asp (debug, v0.1.0) from the repo inside a rust:1-slim-bookworm container (repo mounted read-only, target dir outside the repo) and ran the exact scenario on overlayfs, where non-UTF-8 filenames are legal.

Reproduction (all with the actual asp binary):
1. `asp init`; `echo hello > normal.txt`; `asp checkpoint` → checkpoint #1 OK.
2. `touch caf\351.txt` (raw 0xE9 byte, verified with od: `63 61 66 e9 2e 74 78 74`).
3. `asp checkpoint` → exit 1: "error: git --literal-pathspecs failed: fatal: pathspec 'caf\u{fffd}.txt' did not match any files".
4. Unrelated edit + `asp checkpoint` again → same failure. Every checkpoint is broken while the file exists (the file is always in the changed set, so the no-op fast path never engages; below the 2000-entry threshold at workspace.rs:461 the pathspec path at :464-473 is always taken).
5. Hook path: piped a PostToolUse payload to `asp hook-event` → exit 0, empty stdout, only a stderr note (hooks.rs:27-31 swallows the error by design). `asp log` confirms no checkpoint landed — auto-checkpointing silently stops while the agent keeps editing.
6. WORSE THAN CLAIMED: `asp restore 1` also fails (exit 1, same error) because restore takes a safety checkpoint first (workspace.rs:720-724). So undo is unavailable in BOTH directions, not merely the deletion-skip the reviewer described.
7. `asp doctor` → "workspace is healthy" (exit 0). I read all six doctor checks (workspace.rs:1292-1460): journal CRC, refs, journal-vs-refs, fork registry, torn clones, CAS integrity — nothing about filenames. The git stderr does include the lossy name 'caf\u{fffd}.txt', but with the replacement character, no indication it is a filename-encoding problem, and run_with_stdin (gitx.rs:94-102) attaches no corrective hint at all (not even the "run `asp doctor`" hint that run() adds).

Root cause verified in code: scan_changes() lossy-converts status -z paths at crates/asp-core/src/workspace.rs:546 (also :551 for rename sources, :288 in status(), :756 in the restore deletion loop), then stage_tree() feeds the corrupted pathspec to `git --literal-pathspecs add -A --pathspec-from-file=- --pathspec-file-nul` (workspace.rs:464-473); git exits 128 (also reproduced standalone on macOS in a real asp workspace), which run_with_stdin turns into GitFailed and checkpoint() propagates.

Platform scoping verified: creating b'caf\xe9.txt' on macOS APFS fails with EILSEQ (Errno 92), so macOS is unaffected; Linux (a launch platform) is exposed. Property tests only generate `[a-zA-Z0-9][a-zA-Z0-9_.-]*` names (crates/asp-core/tests/properties.rs:85), so the suite cannot catch this. The only non-lossy UTF-8 handling in the codebase is journal.rs:174.

Severity: major is right. Trigger is uncommon (a tarball/zip extraction with latin-1/Shift-JIS names, or an agent bash command) but once triggered the product's core guarantee — always-on undo — is silently off via the hook path, and restore is hard-broken until the file is renamed, with no diagnostic naming the cause and doctor reporting healthy. Not critical: Linux-only, no existing checkpoints are corrupted, and recovery is a rename. Suggested fix as the reviewer proposed: keep paths as raw bytes (Vec<u8>/OsString) from `status -z` through the pathspec stdin and restore's remove_file; or minimally detect U+FFFD after conversion and fail with a hint naming the file, plus a doctor check.

Environment note: to run the Linux repro I started Docker Desktop and freed its wedged disk (100% full) by pruning only unused/inactive Docker build cache (`docker builder prune`, ~21GB, regenerable); repro containers/images and /tmp experiments were removed afterwards. The repo itself was untouched.

confirmed.title: fork_compare (asp forks / race results) runs stage_tree on each fork without the fork's lock — mutates the fork's index/excludes/bigfiles and races its checkpoints, surfacing raw git index.lock errors with actively wrong advice
confirmed.description: diff() against the working tree correctly takes the StoreLock before stage_tree (workspace.rs:1105), but fork_compare() calls `fork_ws.stage_tree()` (workspace.rs:1057) with no lock on the fork, despite stage_tree writing the fork's shadow.index, info/exclude, and bigfiles.json. The documented workflow is agents running inside forks (auto-checkpointing via hooks) while the user/parent runs `asp forks` or `asp race` to compare (race.rs:110 calls fork_compare right when lanes finish, while lane-spawned background processes may still be checkpointing). When the two collide, the user gets git's raw lock error telling them to delete shadow.index.lock manually — advice that is wrong (another asp process legitimately holds it) and dangerous mid-checkpoint. The reverse interleaving can make the fork's own hook checkpoint fail instead. Either way the trust-model promise that errors carry corrective hints is broken in the product's headline workflow.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced: fork with a loop of `asp checkpoint` running inside it while the parent loops `asp forks` → parent fails with: "error: git --literal-pathspecs failed: fatal: Unable to create '/private/tmp/asp-exp5/proj@lane1/.asp/shadow.index.lock': File exists. Another git process seems to be running... remove the file manually to continue." Comment on fork_compare claims it is "Non-committal: measures each fork's current tree without creating checkpoints" yet stage_tree mutates index, excludes, and bigfiles.json.
confirmed.suggested_fix: In fork_compare, acquire each fork's StoreLock before stage_tree (try-lock with a short bounded retry; on persistent contention emit a row marked "busy" rather than failing the whole table). Alternatively make the comparison genuinely read-only: copy the fork's shadow.index to a temp file, run stage_tree against a scoped Shadow with that temp index (as targeted restore already does at workspace.rs:807-812), and skip bigfiles.json/exclude writes in that mode.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: Confirmed by code reading and live reproduction with target/release/asp. fork_compare (crates/asp-core/src/workspace.rs:1057) runs fork_ws.stage_tree() without acquiring the fork's StoreLock, while every other working-tree stage_tree caller (checkpoint at 342/349, diff at 1105) takes the lock first; stage_tree (433-519) writes the fork's shadow.index, info/exclude, and bigfiles.json. Reproduced in /tmp: a checkpoint loop inside a fork vs an `asp forks` loop in the parent produced (a) 8/120 parent failures with git's raw error telling the user to manually delete /private/tmp/.../proj@lane1/.asp/shadow.index.lock — wrong and dangerous advice since a live asp checkpoint holds it, violating the stated trust-model promise that errors carry corrective hints; and (b) 11/200 failures of the fork's own checkpoint despite it holding its StoreLock, which the Claude Code hook path (hooks.rs handle_hook_event, always exits 0) swallows silently — a silently dropped auto-checkpoint/rewind point in the headline workflow (agents auto-checkpointing in forks while user/orchestrator runs `asp forks` or MCP workspace_forks). No persistent corruption (asp doctor healthy afterward, no stale lock), so not critical. Two small overstatements in the claim: race.rs joins lane threads before fork_compare so the race path only races leftover background processes, and the "Non-committal" doc comment only promises no checkpoints; neither changes the verdict. The same unlocked fork stage_tree pattern also exists in discard() at workspace.rs:1253. Suggested fix (per-fork StoreLock with bounded retry, "busy" row on contention) is sound and matches codebase conventions. Severity major is appropriate: reproducible spurious failures with actively misleading recovery advice plus silently lost checkpoints in the product's flagship concurrent workflow, but no data corruption.

confirmed.title: Journal self-heal truncation runs on lock-free read paths — a reader can truncate an entry a concurrent writer just fsynced
confirmed.description: Journal::read() repairs a torn tail by set_len() — but read() is called without the store lock from Workspace::open_root (workspace.rs:172, i.e. every CLI command and every MCP tool call), status()/last_checkpoint (:323), log() (:701), and fork_compare on the fork's journal (:1059). format.md:94 explicitly promises "reads are lock-free", yet this read mutates. Two windows: (1) reader snapshots the file mid-append (single write_all is not read-atomic), classifies the half-visible line as a torn tail, then set_len()s after the writer's write+fsync completed — destroying a durable entry; (2) with a genuinely torn tail from an old crash, the reader computes valid_prefix_len, a locked writer appends a new valid entry after the torn bytes... or more simply between the reader's fs::read and its set_len, and the set_len chops the new entry off. The journal is "the audit log — checkpoint refs are only as trustworthy as their provenance" (journal.rs:103), and hooks append constantly while status/log/MCP reads happen constantly, so the race has real exposure over long sessions. Lost entries also desync next_seq's journal side (refs side compensates) and silently drop restore/promote provenance.
confirmed.evidence: journal.rs:156-160: `let tail_torn = bad.len() == 1 && bad[0].1 >= valid_prefix_len; if tail_torn && bad[0].1 == valid_prefix_len { let f = OpenOptions::new().write(true).open(&self.path)?; f.set_len(valid_prefix_len as u64)?; ... }` — no lock taken. Callers confirmed lock-free: workspace.rs:172 (open_root), :323 (status), :701 (log), :1059 (fork_compare). Writers (journal.append under checkpoint's StoreLock) are not protected against the unlocked reader's set_len.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Only physically truncate while holding the StoreLock: pass a flag (or a &StoreLock) into read(), or have read() try_lock the store lock and skip the set_len when it cannot get it — a torn tail can always be ignored in memory (parse_line already drops it from entries), so healing can safely be deferred to the next locked mutation (checkpoint/append already runs under the lock and could heal first).
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: CONFIRMED by code inspection and concrete reproduction against the real crate. Code facts: Journal::read() (crates/asp-core/src/journal.rs:156-160) does fs::read, parses, then set_len+sync_data with no lock; it is called lock-free from Workspace::open_root (workspace.rs:172 — every CLI command, every MCP tool call via mcp.rs:250, every hook via hooks.rs:62), status/last_checkpoint (:323), log (:701), fork_compare (:1059). Writers append under the exclusive advisory StoreLock (workspace.rs:342→:409; store.rs:158-180), which readers never take, so the lock cannot prevent the reader's truncation. format.md:94 explicitly states 'All mutations take the exclusive advisory lock; reads are lock-free' — yet this read mutates. Reproduction (/tmp/jrace, path-dependency on asp-core, real read()/append()): with a pre-existing torn tail (the product's headline kill -9 scenario) and two concurrent opens — B heals then appends a fsynced entry (exactly open_root + checkpoint), A is a lock-free reader staggered 6-22ms (realistic independent process starts) — 21/200 iterations silently destroyed the durable fsynced entry on a 50k-line journal. The loss is silent (file ends fully valid; no corrupt_lines). Timing model verified by measurement: the window width = journal parse time minus fsync time; on this Mac parse(20k lines)=8ms vs F_FULLFSYNC=8ms (window closed, 0/3000 losses), parse(50k)=20ms (window open, losses cluster at stagger ~8.2ms = fsync latency, exactly as predicted). On Linux (fdatasync ~0.1-1ms) the window opens at journals of only a few thousand lines — ordinary usage, since the journal never rotates and hooks append per tool call. Caveat that tempers but does not refute: the steady-state variant (reader observing a half-written live append with no pre-existing torn tail) did NOT reproduce on macOS in 12,000 fsynced appends against a hot reader loop, so the demonstrated trigger requires a prior crash mid-append plus concurrent opens at first heal — a low-probability but first-class scenario for this product. Impact: silently destroys durable, fsynced audit entries; Restore/Fork/Promote/Discard provenance lives only in the journal (workspace.rs:862,983,1001,1222,1270) so the loss is unrecoverable; checkpoint refs/objects (user data) are unaffected and next_seq survives via refs (workspace.rs:640-646), which is why this is major rather than critical. Suggested fix is sound and cheap: perform the physical set_len only while holding (or try_lock-ing) the StoreLock — the torn tail is already excluded from in-memory entries by parse_line, so healing can be skipped on contention and deferred to the next locked mutation, which should heal before appending.

confirmed.title: store_in_cas hashes the file before cloning it — a concurrent write poisons an immutable CAS entry and silently breaks byte-identical restore
confirmed.description: store_in_cas() runs hash_file(file) (a streaming read that can take seconds on multi-GB files) and then clone_file(file, dst). If the file is written between (or during) the two steps — e.g. a build/download still running in the background when a PostToolUse hook checkpoint fires — the CAS stores content that does not match its BLAKE3 name. The manifest for that checkpoint records the stale hash, so restoring it materializes the wrong bytes with no error, violating crash-invariant 1's byte-identical guarantee (which the torture suite checks, but only without concurrent writers). Because CAS entries are immutable and dedupe is by `dst.exists()`, the poisoned entry is never corrected, and doctor's CAS check (workspace.rs:1439-1466) only tests existence, not content.
confirmed.evidence: blobs.rs:100-107: `let md = file.metadata()?; let hash = hash_file(file)?; let dst = cas_dir.join(&hash); if !dst.exists() { ...; clone_file(file, &dst)?; }` — hash and content are taken from two separate reads of a live file with no verification that they match.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Invert the order to make hash↔content atomic: clone_file the source into a temp name inside the CAS dir first (clonefile/FICLONE is near-instant and snapshots the bytes), hash the clone, then rename it to its hash (atomic, idempotent). Also have `asp doctor` optionally re-hash CAS entries (cheap enough behind a flag) so poisoning from older versions is detectable.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: CONFIRMED by end-to-end reproduction with the release binary. Setup: /tmp/asp-race workspace, blob_threshold_mb=1, 400MB file, background writer rewriting random 1MB regions (simulating an in-progress download/build). `asp checkpoint` during writes succeeded and produced poisoned CAS entries on the FIRST attempt (both entries created: blob named 600d9aac… actually hashes to 9632f427…). Then: (a) `asp doctor` reported "workspace is healthy" — its CAS check at workspace.rs:1441-1442 only tests existence, not content; (b) `asp restore 1` exited 0 with no warning and materialized bytes hashing to 9632f427… while the checkpoint's manifest (refs/asp/meta/1) records blake3 600d9aac… — silent byte-identical-restore violation; the documented stock-tools runbook (`cp .asp/blobs/<hash> <path>`, blobs.rs:6) yields the same wrong bytes. Code confirms the mechanism: blobs.rs:100-107 hashes the live file (~0.5s streaming read on 400MB) then separately clones it; workspace.rs:574,590 call store_in_cas on the live workspace path with no tree freeze; dedupe by `!dst.exists()` (blobs.rs:104) makes the poisoned entry permanent; restore_from_cas (blobs.rs:117-133) never verifies. The suggested fix (clone to temp name in CAS dir, hash the clone, atomic rename to hash; clonefile is an atomic file-level snapshot on APFS) is correct, cheap, and kill-9-safe, plus an optional doctor re-hash flag. Severity: major, not critical — it requires a concurrent writer to a >threshold file at checkpoint time (realistic for hook-driven checkpoints during agent sessions with large downloads/artifacts), never touches the user's live files or user .git, and the capture would be torn under concurrent writes regardless; the bug's harm is that the inconsistency is permanent, silent, and undetectable by doctor, breaking the product's core trust claim for that checkpoint.

confirmed.title: Released Linux binaries are glibc-dynamic (built on glibc 2.39), contradicting the "static binary" claim and failing on most distros
confirmed.description: README.md:18 says "asp is that safety net, as one static binary" and v1-brief repeats "a single static binary", but the release matrix builds x86_64/aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu on ubuntu-latest (24.04, glibc 2.39) and ubuntu-24.04-arm with no crt-static or musl configuration anywhere (no .cargo/config.toml, nothing in Cargo.toml). Rust gnu targets link glibc dynamically, and glibc symbol versioning means the artifact requires glibc >= the build machine's. The launch binaries will abort with "version `GLIBC_2.39' not found" on Ubuntu 22.04 (2.35), Debian 12 (2.36), RHEL 9 (2.34), and will not run at all on Alpine/musl — yet install.sh maps every `uname -s = Linux` to the gnu tarball with no libc detection. For a trust-first storage tool launch, the install path failing on the majority of current LTS distros is the first impression.
confirmed.evidence: release.yml: `- target: x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu\n  os: ubuntu-latest` (builds against glibc 2.39); install.sh:20 `Linux) os="unknown-linux-gnu" ;;` unconditionally; README.md:18 "as one static binary"; workspace has no .cargo/config.toml and Cargo.toml has no `crt-static`/musl config — confirmed by inspection.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Switch the Linux release targets to x86_64/aarch64-unknown-linux-musl (truly static, matches the README claim, one artifact covers glibc and musl distros), update install.sh to download the musl asset, and add a release-job smoke test that runs the produced binary in an old-glibc container (e.g. debian:11). If musl is undesirable (blake3 perf), build gnu artifacts against an old glibc (zigbuild/cross with glibc 2.17) and delete the word "static" from README/v1-brief.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: Fully reproduced empirically. (1) Config facts verified: release.yml:21-24 builds x86_64/aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu on ubuntu-latest/ubuntu-24.04-arm (glibc 2.39); no .cargo/config.toml exists and no crt-static/musl config anywhere in the repo; install.sh:20 unconditionally maps Linux to the gnu tarball; README.md:18 and docs/design/v1-brief.md:35 both claim a "static binary". (2) Built the exact CI artifact in an ubuntu:24.04 container (glibc 2.39, rustc 1.96.0 stable, cargo build --release -p asp, mirroring the ubuntu-24.04-arm lane): the binary is dynamically linked (NEEDED libc.so.6, libgcc_s.so.1) — the "static" claim is false — and its .gnu.version_r carries a HARD (Flags: none) GLIBC_2.39 version requirement, introduced by Rust std's weak pidfd_spawn* references whose version-need the linker does not mark weak. (3) Ran the artifact across distros: ubuntu:22.04 (glibc 2.35), debian:12 (2.36), and rockylinux:9/RHEL9 (2.34) all abort with "version 'GLIBC_2.39' not found"; debian:11 and ubuntu:20.04 (2.31) fail with multiple missing versions; alpine:3.20 (musl) fails with "exec: no such file or directory"; only ubuntu:24.04 works. Even the claim's strongest assertion (failure on 22.04/Debian 12/RHEL 9 with that exact error) is literally accurate. Release CI runs tests only on the build machine, so nothing would catch this before launch. Severity stays major: the documented install path fails on the majority of currently-supported LTS distros at first run, and the headline "static binary" claim is false — a launch-blocking install/trust problem, though not data loss or a security hole (and building from source remains a workaround). Fix as suggested: switch Linux release targets to musl (truly static, covers glibc+musl), update install.sh, and add an old-glibc (e.g. debian:11) container smoke test to the release job; alternatively build gnu artifacts against an old glibc via cargo-zigbuild/cross and remove "static" from README.md:18 and v1-brief.md:35.

confirmed.title: Torn fork clones without .asp are invisible to doctor and unremovable by asp — documented kill-9 invariant 3 is false, especially on Linux
confirmed.description: format.md:97-101 and README.md:67 promise "a torn fork (clone died mid-flight) is detectable... and removable by asp doctor" / "doctor --fix repairs anything torn". Doctor's torn-fork scan skips any `<dirname>@<name>` sibling that lacks an `.asp` dir (`if !candidate.asp.exists() { continue; }`). On Linux, `reflink_walk` is a user-space per-file walk whose `read_dir` order is arbitrary, so a kill -9 (or any mid-walk error: EACCES on a root-owned file in a Docker bind-mount, ENOSPC, ENAMETOOLONG on a >PATH_MAX-deep tree) routinely leaves a partial clone where `.asp` was never copied. `clone_tree`/`reflink_walk` also do no cleanup on the error path (fork.rs:84-116), so the partial tree persists. Demonstrated on macOS: doctor reports "workspace is healthy", `asp fork --name partial` fails with ForkExists whose hint says to `asp discard` it, and `asp discard partial` fails with ForkNotFound — a recovery dead-end that contradicts the trust model. The torture test kill9_fork_leaves_no_unrecoverable_state passes only because macOS clonefile is a single kernel syscall; it would not necessarily hold on Linux CI (the btrfs job does run it, but the assertion only checks doctor output, which is exactly what's blind here).
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced: `mkdir -p /tmp/aspenv/proj@partial/src` (no .asp inside) → `asp doctor --fix` → "✓ workspace is healthy"; `asp fork --name partial` → "error: destination already exists... hint: pick a different fork name, or `asp discard` the old fork first"; `asp discard partial` → "error: no fork named 'partial' to discard". Code: workspace.rs:1397-1399 `if !candidate.asp.exists() { continue; }`; fork.rs reflink_walk propagates errors with no remove_dir_all of the partial dst.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make fork creation atomic to observers: clone into a temp sibling name (e.g. `<parent>/.asp-fork-tmp-<uuid>`), rewrite workspace.json/forks.json inside it, then `rename()` to the final `<dirname>@<name>` (same-volume rename is atomic). Teach doctor to flag/remove `.asp-fork-tmp-*` leftovers unconditionally. Add best-effort `remove_dir_all(dst)` on every clone_tree error path, and extend the ForkExists hint to cover the unregistered-directory case ("if it is not listed in `asp forks`, remove the directory manually").
confirmed.verifier_reasoning:
CONFIRMED — reproduced with the release binary and verified at every cited code location.

1. Doctor blindness reproduced. In /tmp: created workspace `proj`, checkpointed, then simulated a torn fork by creating `proj@partial/src` with no `.asp` inside. `asp doctor --fix` printed "workspace is healthy". Code: crates/asp-core/src/workspace.rs:1397-1399 — `if !candidate.asp.exists() { continue; }` skips any `<dirname>@<name>` sibling lacking `.asp`, so it is neither flagged nor removed.

2. Recovery dead-end reproduced verbatim. `asp fork --name partial` → "error: destination already exists ... hint: pick a different fork name, or `asp discard` the old fork first" (ForkExists raised in fork.rs:27-33). `asp discard partial` → "error: no fork named 'partial' to discard" (workspace.rs:1242-1243, registry-based). The hint points to a command that cannot succeed; the fork name is permanently unusable through asp and only manual rm -rf clears it. This directly contradicts trust-model item 4 (errors carry corrective hints).

3. The state is reachable on Linux. fork.rs:70-116: `platform_clone` on Linux is `reflink_walk`, a user-space per-entry recursion over `read_dir` (arbitrary order — `.asp` is not guaranteed to be copied first). kill -9 between syscalls, or any propagated error (EACCES, ENOSPC — `?` at fork.rs:90-107 with no `remove_dir_all(dst)`; caller workspace.rs:954 also propagates without cleanup), leaves a partial dst that may lack `.asp`. Same shape for `copy_recursive` (macOS ENOTSUP fallback and other-OS fallback, fork.rs:118-147). I verified no other code path cleans partial clones.

4. Why macOS torture passes (and the claim's mechanism is right): I ran a kill -9 sweep (15 kills, delays 2.0-11.5s) against `asp fork` on an 80,000-file tree. Every kill left a COMPLETE clone (80,033 files, `.asp` present) carrying the parent's workspace id — clonefile is a single uninterruptible kernel syscall, so SIGKILL only lands after the clone finishes, leaving exactly the "fixup never ran" state doctor handles (workspace.rs:1402-1407 flagged all 15 as torn). The no-.asp window essentially doesn't exist on macOS/APFS; it does on Linux.

5. The Linux CI torture run would not catch it: .github/workflows/ci.yml:34-49 runs the suite on btrfs, but kill9_fork_leaves_no_unrecoverable_state (crates/asp/tests/torture.rs:164-205) asserts only (a) doctor reports no non-info findings after --fix — doctor is blind to no-.asp dirs, so this passes vacuously — and (b) a fork with a FRESH name "survivor" succeeds, never retrying a victim's name, so the ForkExists dead-end is never exercised. (c) `forks` listing is registry-only.

6. Docs claims verified: docs/design/format.md:97-101 invariant 3 "a torn fork (clone died mid-flight) is detectable ... and removable by `asp doctor`" and README.md "`asp doctor --fix` repairs anything torn". On Linux a clone that died mid-flight before `.asp` was copied is neither detectable nor removable by asp. (format.md's parenthetical narrows "torn" to the workspace.json-not-rewritten case, but the README claim and the plain reading of invariant 3 are falsified.)

Severity: major, not critical — no checkpointed data is lost and the parent workspace stays healthy; the damage is a false documented safety invariant, a doctor that reports healthy while a torn artifact persists, a permanently blocked fork name, and a hint chain that dead-ends (especially bad for the MCP/agent loop, where retrying the same fork name after a crash is the natural next action). The suggested fix (clone into a temp sibling, atomic rename to final name, doctor cleans temp leftovers unconditionally, best-effort remove_dir_all on clone error, extend the ForkExists hint) is sound and addresses the false-positive risk that motivated the `.asp` check.

confirmed.title: Promote runs user git with inherited GIT_DIR/GIT_WORK_TREE — exported GIT_DIR makes promote write into an unrelated repo and fail
confirmed.description: The shadow path (gitx.rs:35-48) carefully pins GIT_DIR/GIT_WORK_TREE/GIT_INDEX_FILE, but the user-git path in promote (`run_user_git`, `user_git_ref_exists`, `build_user_commit`) inherits the caller's environment and relies on `git -C` discovery. If the invoking shell exports an absolute GIT_DIR/GIT_WORK_TREE — the popular bare-repo dotfiles pattern (`export GIT_DIR=$HOME/.dotfiles GIT_WORK_TREE=$HOME` in .zshrc), or tools/hooks that export GIT_DIR absolutely — then `git -C <fork> add/write-tree/commit-tree/update-ref` all operate on the *wrong* repository: foreign commit objects and a transient `refs/asp-promote/<fork>` ref are written into the unrelated repo (violating "the user's .git is sacred", CLAUDE.md), and the subsequent fetch fails with a baffling "couldn't find remote ref" error with no hint. The shadow path also fails to scrub GIT_OBJECT_DIRECTORY, GIT_COMMON_DIR, GIT_NAMESPACE, and GIT_CONFIG_COUNT/GIT_CONFIG_KEY_*/GIT_CONFIG_VALUE_* — e.g. a checkpoint fired from inside a git pre-receive hook (quarantine GIT_OBJECT_DIRECTORY) would write shadow objects into a quarantine dir that gets deleted.
