confirmed.evidence: Reproduced: with `export GIT_DIR=/tmp/aspenv/otherrepo/.git`, `asp promote try1` → "error: git fetch failed in /private/tmp/aspenv/proj: fatal: couldn't find remote ref refs/asp-promote/try1"; the update-ref/commit-tree succeeded against otherrepo (run_user_git returned success and the fork's repo has no such ref), proving objects+ref landed in the unrelated $GIT_DIR repo. Code: workspace.rs:1492-1530 (`Command::new("git").arg("-C")...` with no env_remove), build_user_commit workspace.rs:1534-1588 only overrides GIT_INDEX_FILE.
confirmed.suggested_fix: In run_user_git, user_git_ref_exists, and build_user_commit: `env_remove` GIT_DIR, GIT_WORK_TREE, GIT_INDEX_FILE (except the one explicitly set), GIT_OBJECT_DIRECTORY, GIT_COMMON_DIR, GIT_NAMESPACE before spawning, so `-C` discovery always wins while user *config* still applies. In Shadow::command, additionally scrub GIT_OBJECT_DIRECTORY/GIT_ALTERNATE_OBJECT_DIRECTORIES/GIT_COMMON_DIR/GIT_NAMESPACE/GIT_CONFIG_COUNT (simplest: remove every inherited `GIT_*` var except GIT_EXEC_PATH, then set the pinned ones).
confirmed.verifier_reasoning:
CONFIRMED — reproduced both the primary and secondary effects with the release binary.

Code evidence: /Users/apple/Projects/agentspaces/crates/asp-core/src/workspace.rs:1492-1513 (`run_user_git`), 1515-1530 (`user_git_ref_exists`), and 1534-1588 (`build_user_commit`) all spawn `git -C <dir>` with the caller's environment fully inherited — no `env_remove` of GIT_DIR/GIT_WORK_TREE/etc. (`build_user_commit` only sets GIT_INDEX_FILE, line 1542). By contrast, the shadow path (crates/asp-core/src/gitx.rs:35-49, `Shadow::command`) deliberately env_removes and re-pins GIT_DIR/GIT_WORK_TREE/GIT_INDEX_FILE — proving the env-inheritance in the user-git path is an oversight, not a design choice. Since GIT_DIR in the environment takes precedence over `-C` discovery, an exported absolute GIT_DIR redirects every user-git operation to the wrong repository. Neither the CLI nor asp-core scrubs git env vars anywhere else (grep confirmed).

Reproduction 1 (promote, primary claim): created /tmp proj (git repo + asp workspace + fork try1 with edits) and an unrelated repo `other`. With `export GIT_DIR=$D/other/.git`, `asp -C $D/proj promote try1` failed with exactly the claimed baffling error: "error: git fetch failed in .../proj: fatal: couldn't find remote ref refs/asp-promote/try1" (no hint). The unrelated repo's loose-object count went from 3 to 49; `git fsck --dangling` in `other` shows a dangling commit "asp: promote fork 'try1'" authored with the user's real global identity, containing the fork's entire tree including .asp/ internals (shadow.git config, journal.jsonl, etc.). The transient refs/asp-promote/try1 was created and then deleted in the wrong repo, leaving dangling foreign objects behind. Control run with clean env succeeded ("✓ fork try1 landed as branch asp/try1"). This violates trust-model pillar 3 ("user .git is sacred") for whatever repo GIT_DIR points at, leaks workspace content (potentially secrets) into an unrelated repo's object store, and pillar 4 (the error carries no corrective hint).

Reproduction 2 (shadow path, secondary claim): with `export GIT_OBJECT_DIRECTORY=/tmp/quarantine` (the env git sets for pre-receive quarantine), `asp checkpoint -m test` reported success ("✓ checkpoint #1") but ALL shadow objects landed in the quarantine dir — .asp/shadow.git/objects contains zero loose objects. After deleting the quarantine dir (as git does when a push is rejected), the checkpoint is unrecoverable: `asp undo` fails with "error: git status failed: fatal: bad object HEAD" — the shadow repo's HEAD references an object that no longer exists. That is silent data loss in the flagship guarantee (trust-model pillar 1: "every checkpoint recoverable with stock git"). Shadow::command (gitx.rs:35-49) indeed fails to scrub GIT_OBJECT_DIRECTORY, GIT_COMMON_DIR, GIT_NAMESPACE, GIT_ALTERNATE_OBJECT_DIRECTORIES, and GIT_CONFIG_COUNT/KEY/VALUE.

Severity assessment: "major" is right, not "critical" — a default shell environment is unaffected, and the foreign-repo damage is pollution (dangling objects + transient ref) rather than destruction of reachable history. But it is not minor either: asp's whole audience is agents invoked from wrapper scripts, hooks, and CI, and git itself exports these vars when running hooks, `git rebase -x`, `git bisect run`, etc. — exactly the contexts agent tooling runs in (asp even ships Claude Code hook integration). When it triggers, it silently breaks two of the four trust-model pillars and produces a hintless, baffling failure. The suggested fix is correct and a few lines: env_remove the repo-location GIT_* vars (GIT_DIR, GIT_WORK_TREE, GIT_INDEX_FILE except where explicitly set, GIT_OBJECT_DIRECTORY, GIT_COMMON_DIR, GIT_NAMESPACE, GIT_ALTERNATE_OBJECT_DIRECTORIES) in run_user_git/user_git_ref_exists/build_user_commit so `-C` discovery wins while user config still applies, and scrub the same set (plus GIT_CONFIG_COUNT/KEY_*/VALUE_*) in Shadow::command before pinning.

confirmed.title: Relative -C / MCP `directory` paths break every shadow git operation with a misleading hint
confirmed.description: `Workspace::open` never canonicalizes the discovered root (only `init` does), so a relative `-C` argument or MCP `directory` value (mcp.rs:253-263 does `PathBuf::from(d)` verbatim) produces relative GIT_DIR/GIT_WORK_TREE/GIT_INDEX_FILE env values. Git resolves a relative GIT_DIR against the child's cwd, which Shadow sets to the work tree — so the path resolves to `<root>/<relative-root>/.asp/shadow.git`, which only accidentally works when the relative path is exactly `../<dirname>`. Every command fails with "fatal: not a git repository: '../../proj/.asp/shadow.git'" and the generic hint "run `asp doctor` to check workspace health" — but doctor fails the same way, a scary dead-end for a storage tool whose primary users are agents that routinely pass relative paths.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced: `cd /tmp/aspenv/elsewhere/deep && asp -C ../../proj status` → "error: git status failed: fatal: not a git repository: '../../proj/.asp/shadow.git'" (and `asp -C ../../proj doctor` would fail identically), while `asp -C ../proj status` from /tmp/aspenv/elsewhere happens to work because `proj/../proj == proj`. Code: main.rs:215-221 returns the -C path as given; workspace.rs:149-180 open_root builds Layout from it; gitx.rs:38-40 passes the relative paths as env.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Canonicalize once at the boundary: in `Workspace::open_root`, `let root = root.canonicalize()?` (mirroring init at workspace.rs:184), or canonicalize in main.rs `cwd()` and mcp.rs `directory_arg()`. One line, fixes CLI and MCP simultaneously.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: Confirmed by code inspection and live reproduction with target/release/asp (v0.1.0). Code: main.rs:215-221 returns the -C path verbatim; mcp.rs:253-263 does PathBuf::from(directory) verbatim; workspace.rs:149 open_root never canonicalizes (the only canonicalize in the workspace is init at workspace.rs:184, verified by grep); gitx.rs:38-48 exports the relative root as GIT_DIR/GIT_WORK_TREE/GIT_INDEX_FILE while setting the child's cwd to the work tree, so git resolves the relative GIT_DIR against the work tree instead of the launch dir. Repro: from /tmp/asp_relc_verify/elsewhere/deep, `asp -C ../../proj status` fails with "fatal: not a git repository: '../../proj/.asp/shadow.git'" (exit 1); `asp -C ../../proj doctor` fails identically and its error even carries the hint "run `asp doctor`..." — a dead-end. MCP workspace_status with directory="../../proj" returns the same isError result. The claimed accidental-success case (`-C ../proj` from one level up) also reproduces. Additionally found a worse variant of the same root cause: a relative -C path with enough `..` components from inside a workspace makes find_root (store.rs:103) walk up to the empty path, match the process cwd's workspace as root "", and fail with the false error "git is not installed or not on PATH". No docs/help/schema text requires absolute paths, and git -C (the obvious model) accepts relative paths. Suggested fix is correct and one line; canonicalizing at the boundaries (cwd() and directory_arg()) is slightly better than inside open_root since it also cleans up the empty-root edge. Severity stays major: no data loss (fails before writing), but every operation breaks on a routine input with misleading errors and a broken escape hatch, and the primary users are agents.

confirmed.title: A single non-UTF8 filename (valid on Linux) permanently breaks checkpointing — hooks then fail silently, disabling the safety net
confirmed.description: scan_changes decodes `git status -z` path bytes with `String::from_utf8_lossy`, replacing invalid bytes with U+FFFD. stage_tree then feeds those lossy strings to `git --literal-pathspecs add -A --pathspec-from-file=- --pathspec-file-nul` (workspace.rs:461-474). The lossy pathspec no longer matches the on-disk name, and git exits 128 ("pathspec ... did not match any files"), so the whole checkpoint errors. Linux filesystems happily hold non-UTF8 names — agents create them constantly by extracting latin-1 zips or scraping files. Once one exists, *every* checkpoint fails until it is deleted, and because `asp hook-event` deliberately swallows errors (hooks.rs:27-31, exit 0 with a stderr note Claude Code users never see), the auto-checkpoint safety net is silently off while the user believes they are protected. macOS is unaffected (APFS enforces UTF-8), so the macOS-only dev loop and torture suite never see it; the >2000-file full-scan path (`add -A .`) handles these names fine, which proves the capture model itself is compatible.
confirmed.evidence: Failure mode reproduced byte-for-byte: `printf 'nonexistent\xef\xbf\xbd.txt' | git --literal-pathspecs add -A --pathspec-from-file=- --pathspec-file-nul` (with the shadow env) → "fatal: pathspec 'nonexistent�.txt' did not match any files", exit 128 → Shadow::run_with_stdin → GitFailed → checkpoint returns Err. Lossy decode at workspace.rs:546 `String::from_utf8_lossy(&entry[3..]).to_string()`; same at line 288 (status) and 551 (rename source).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Keep paths as bytes end-to-end: store `Vec<u8>`/`OsString` in ScanResult, change run_with_stdin to take `&[u8]`, and write raw NUL-separated bytes to --pathspec-from-file (git accepts raw bytes there). Cheaper stopgap: detect any non-UTF8 path in the changed set and fall back to the `add -A .` full-scan path for that capture. Add a Linux-only test creating `OsStr::from_bytes(b"caf\xe9.txt")` and asserting checkpoint succeeds.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: Reproduced end-to-end on Linux (docker, git 2.54): a non-UTF8 filename (b'caf\xe9.txt') appears as raw bytes in `git status --porcelain -z -uall`; workspace.rs:546 lossy-decodes it to caf\u{FFFD}.txt; the exact command from workspace.rs:464-473 (`git --literal-pathspecs add -A --pathspec-from-file=- --pathspec-file-nul`) exits 128 with "fatal: pathspec 'caf�.txt' did not match any files"; gitx.rs:94-103 converts that to Err(GitFailed); checkpoint_locked (workspace.rs:349) propagates with no fallback. The file stays untracked so every subsequent checkpoint fails until it is deleted. hooks.rs:27-31 + main.rs:629-630 swallow the error with exit 0, so the Claude Code auto-checkpoint safety net is silently disabled. Blast radius is wider than claimed: stage_tree is also called from fork/promote paths (workspace.rs:1057, 1106, 1253). Verified macOS APFS rejects such names (EILSEQ), explaining why the macOS dev loop never sees it; CI runs ubuntu but no test creates a non-UTF8 name. The >2000-file full-scan path (`git add -A .`) handles the same file fine (verified, exit 0, file indexed), so the suggested stopgap fallback is sound. Severity stays major: no existing data is corrupted and user .git is untouched, but the advertised always-on safety net silently turns off on Linux, the primary agent platform — a direct trust-model violation for v0.1.0.

confirmed.title: "git >= 2.30" requirement is wrong: config isolation needs GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL/GIT_CONFIG_SYSTEM (git 2.32+), and no runtime version check exists
confirmed.description: README.md:97 and install.sh:14 state git >= 2.30, but the trust-critical isolation of the shadow repo from user/global config is implemented solely via the GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL/GIT_CONFIG_SYSTEM environment variables (gitx.rs:41-42 and 127-128), which git only honors from 2.32 (June 2021). On git 2.30/2.31 — exactly what Debian 11 and early RHEL 9 ship — those vars are silently ignored and the user's global/system config applies to every shadow operation: `core.autocrlf=true` rewrites file bytes through add/checkout-index (breaking the torture suite's "byte-identical restores" guarantee), `core.fsmonitor` spawns watchman per command, `init.templateDir` seeds shadow.git with arbitrary template content. There is also no `git --version` check anywhere (init, doctor, or install.sh, which only warns when git is entirely absent), so users on genuinely old git (CentOS 7's 1.8, Ubuntu 20.04's 2.25 — below even the claimed minimum, and lacking `--pathspec-from-file`, git 2.25+) fail deep inside a checkpoint with "git add failed: unknown option" and the unhelpful hint "run asp doctor".
confirmed.evidence: gitx.rs:41-42 `.env("GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL", null_device()).env("GIT_CONFIG_SYSTEM", null_device())` is the only config isolation mechanism; both env vars were introduced in git 2.32.0 (git release notes / git(1) docs). `grep -rn "version"` across crates shows no git version probe; install.sh:14 only checks `command -v git`. stage_tree's `--pathspec-from-file`/`--pathspec-file-nul` (workspace.rs:466-470) additionally requires git 2.25+.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Bump the documented minimum to git 2.32 in README and install.sh, and add a one-time `git --version` probe in `Workspace::init` and `doctor` that errors/warns with a corrective hint when below minimum. For defense in depth on the shadow path, also set `GIT_CONFIG_NOSYSTEM=1` (supported since 1.6) alongside GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL so system config stays out even on older git.
confirmed.verifier_reasoning: Every element of the claim verified against code, git's official release notes, and an empirical reproduction with the release binary. (1) README.md:97 and install.sh:14 document git >= 2.30; install.sh only checks `command -v git`. (2) Shadow-repo config isolation is implemented solely via GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL/GIT_CONFIG_SYSTEM at gitx.rs:41-42 (every shadow op) and gitx.rs:127-128 (init --bare); no GIT_CONFIG_NOSYSTEM or HOME pinning exists anywhere. (3) Git 2.32.0 release notes confirm both env vars were introduced in 2.32 ("Replace GIT_CONFIG_NOSYSTEM mechanism ... with GIT_CONFIG_SYSTEM ... and introduce GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL"); pre-2.32 git silently ignores them. (4) grep confirms no `git --version` probe in either crate; Workspace::doctor (workspace.rs:1292) checks journal/refs/forks only. (5) Reproduced concretely in /tmp: a git shim stripping the two env vars (exactly modeling pre-2.32 behavior) plus a user ~/.gitconfig with core.autocrlf=true caused `asp restore` to return CRLF bytes for an LF file (sha256 bce2ae... -> a4d18c...), violating the byte-identical-restore trust guarantee; control run with the vars honored restored byte-identically under the same hostile config. So on git 2.30/2.31 (Debian 11, early RHEL 9 — exactly the documented minimum) user global/system config silently applies to all shadow operations. Severity stays major rather than critical: modern git (>= 2.32, June 2021) is unaffected so most users are fine, but the documented minimum is factually wrong, the failure is silent data corruption of the product's headline guarantee, and the fix (bump docs to 2.32, add a version probe in init/doctor, set GIT_CONFIG_NOSYSTEM=1 as defense in depth) is trivial and launch-appropriate.

minors.title: fork_compare and discard's guard mutate a fork's store (shadow index, info/exclude, bigfiles.json) without taking the fork's lock
minors.description: fork_compare (the `asp forks` table) calls fork_ws.stage_tree() on each active fork with NO StoreLock on the fork (and none on the parent either); discard's unpromoted-work guard does the same at workspace.rs:1254 while holding only the parent's lock. stage_tree writes the fork's shadow.index, rewrites shadow.git/info/exclude, and can rewrite bigfiles.json. This contradicts docs/design/format.md:94 ('All mutations take the exclusive advisory lock... reads are lock-free') and races with the headline `asp race` flow: a hook-driven checkpoint inside the fork and a parent `asp forks` run concurrently -> git index.lock contention makes one side fail with GitFailed mid-agent-run, and interleaved write_excludes/save_bigfiles can momentarily clobber the fork's big-file bookkeeping (worst observed consequence is a spurious error or a multi-GB file staged into the fork's shadow object store on the >2000-file full-scan path; state self-corrects on the next scan). Verified that the lock is simply not honored: with the fork's .asp/lock held exclusively by another process, `asp forks` proceeds and the fork's shadow.index mtime changes.
minors.evidence: Held fcntl exclusive lock on proj@busy/.asp/lock from a background process; `asp forks` in the parent succeeded and the fork's .asp/shadow.index mtime changed (before=1781127984 after=1781127985). Code: workspace.rs:1057 `let (tree, _) = fork_ws.stage_tree()?;` with no StoreLock::acquire on fork_ws; same at workspace.rs:1254 in discard().
minors.suggested_fix: Acquire the fork's StoreLock (try_lock; on Locked, report the fork as 'busy' in the compare row rather than blocking) before stage_tree in fork_compare and in discard's guard. Alternatively make compare genuinely read-only: diff the fork's worktree against the base with a throwaway temp index (as targeted restore already does) so compare mutates nothing.

minors.title: Journal torn-tail self-heal runs on lock-free read paths and can truncate a concurrent writer's entry
minors.description: Journal::read() truncates the file (set_len) whenever the final line looks torn. read() is called from lock-free paths — Workspace::open_root (workspace.rs:172), status()->last_checkpoint, log(). If a reader snapshots the file mid-append (writer holds the store lock; reader takes none), the partial final line is indistinguishable from a crash artifact and the reader truncates it AFTER the writer's write+fsync completed — deleting a valid journal entry (provenance, which the docs call 'the audit log... only as trustworthy as their provenance'). The window is narrow (appends are single small write(2) calls) but the trust model is 'kill -9 at any line', and this is the same file the CRC machinery exists to protect.
minors.evidence: journal.rs:156-161: `let tail_torn = bad.len() == 1 && bad[0].1 >= valid_prefix_len; if tail_torn && bad[0].1 == valid_prefix_len { let f = OpenOptions::new().write(true).open(&self.path)?; f.set_len(valid_prefix_len as u64)?; ... }` — executed by any reader, no lock. Callers: workspace.rs:172 (open), 323 (status), 701 (log).
minors.suggested_fix: Only truncate while holding the StoreLock: have read() report the torn tail without healing, and perform the set_len in a separate heal step invoked from paths that already hold the lock (checkpoint/restore/fork) or that acquire it just for the heal after re-reading the file. A reader that cannot get the lock should just skip the unparsable tail line.

minors.title: Non-UTF-8 filenames are mangled by from_utf8_lossy, making every subsequent checkpoint fail (Linux)
minors.description: scan_changes parses `git status -z` raw bytes but converts paths with String::from_utf8_lossy (workspace.rs:546, 551; also status() at 288). A file whose name is not valid UTF-8 (legal on Linux ext4/btrfs — e.g. extracted from a latin-1 tarball) becomes a replacement-character string; the literal pathspec then matches nothing and `git add --pathspec-from-file` fails ('did not match any files'), so checkpoint returns GitFailed on every attempt until the user finds and renames the file — the hint ('run asp doctor') does not identify the cause, and hooks fail silently in the background. Not reproducible on macOS (APFS enforces UTF-8), so the suite never sees it; the OSS launch audience includes Linux.
minors.evidence: workspace.rs:546 `let path = String::from_utf8_lossy(&entry[3..]).to_string();` feeding workspace.rs:464-473 `add --pathspec-from-file=- --pathspec-file-nul` with `--literal-pathspecs`; an invalid-UTF-8 name cannot round-trip through the lossy conversion, so the pathspec cannot match the on-disk file and git add exits non-zero, aborting checkpoint_locked.
minors.suggested_fix: Keep paths as raw bytes end-to-end: store Vec<u8>/OsString in ScanResult and write the raw bytes into the pathspec-from-file stdin (it is NUL-separated already, so no encoding is needed). Where a String is unavoidable (JSON manifests), reject or percent-encode non-UTF-8 names with a clear error naming the offending file.

minors.title: Two error hints print literal un-interpolated {fork_name}/{name} placeholders
minors.description: Both hints were written as plain strings, not format! calls, so the user (and any agent that follows hints literally — the stated design goal) is told to run `asp promote {fork_name}` / `asp discard {name}` verbatim. Triggered by `asp discard <fork-with-work>` and `asp fork --name <existing>`.
minors.evidence: Observed output: 'hint: promote it first (`asp promote {fork_name}`) or pass --force to delete the work' and 'hint: pick another name, or `asp discard {name}` first'. Code: `.with_hint("pick another name, or `asp discard {name}` first")` (line 939) and `.with_hint("promote it first (`asp promote {fork_name}`) ...")` (line 1259) — no format!.
minors.suggested_fix: Wrap both in format! so the real fork name is interpolated; add a test that no emitted hint contains '{'.

minors.title: Promote success message hardcodes `git diff main...` — wrong command on master/other default branches
minors.description: After promote, asp prints 'review it: git diff main...asp/<fork>'. In a repo whose default branch is master (git's out-of-box default without init.defaultBranch configured) the suggested command fails with 'unknown revision'. I hit this on my first promote. The README quickstart's mental model ('promote the winner, review like a PR') breaks at the literal first suggested command.
minors.evidence: Reproduced in a `git init` repo (branch master): promote printed 'review it: git diff main...asp/fork-2'; running it → 'fatal: ambiguous argument main...asp/fork-2: unknown revision'. Code: `ui::cyan(&format!("git diff main...{}", report.branch))`.
minors.suggested_fix: Resolve the repo's current branch (`git symbolic-ref --short HEAD`, falling back to 'main') and interpolate it into both the diff and merge suggestions.

minors.title: why-not-git-worktrees.md cites stale spike numbers that contradict BENCHMARKS.md
minors.description: The doc claims '`asp fork` ≈ 0.9s ... `git worktree add` took 13.8s'. The current measured numbers (docs/benchmarks/BENCHMARKS.md, same tree) are asp fork 1224 ms and git worktree add 9.8 s; 0.9s was the raw clonefile(2) syscall spike (spike-results.md), not `asp fork` end-to-end, and 13.8s is an older worktree run. As written, the comparison flatters asp by ~0.3s and penalizes worktrees by 4s — a bad look for a launch whose README leads with 'Honest numbers'. README itself (lines 20, 79) matches BENCHMARKS.md correctly.
minors.evidence: why-not-git-worktrees.md line 9: 'asp fork ≈ 0.9s and 32 MB of disk; git worktree add took 13.8s'. BENCHMARKS.md (2026-06-11): 'fork (whole tree, CoW) 1224 ms', 'git worktree add baseline (tracked only) 9.8 s'.
minors.suggested_fix: Update the line to the BENCHMARKS.md numbers (~1.2s vs ~10s) or explicitly label the 0.9s/13.8s figures as the raw clonefile spike with a link to spike-results.md.

minors.title: Promoted fork's directory lingers on disk and disappears from `asp forks`; quickstart never cleans up the winner
minors.description: After `asp promote race-2`, the fork's status becomes Promoted: it no longer appears in `asp forks` or the `active forks` count, but `<parent>/proj@race-2` (a full tree clone) remains on disk. The README quickstart discards race-1 and race-3 but never race-2, so a user following it literally ends with an orphan directory that asp's own listing no longer shows — the only discovery path is `ls ..`. Nothing in promote's output, the README, or the FAQ says the directory survives promotion or that `asp discard <promoted-fork>` (which works, without --force) is the cleanup step. Related gap: no doc mentions that forks land as `<dirname>@<fork>` siblings of your project at all (only docs/design/format.md says so).
minors.evidence: Reproduced: `asp promote fork-2` → `asp forks` lists only fork-1/fork-3; `ls /tmp/asp-ux` still shows proj@fork-2. README:45-46 promotes race-2 then discards only race-1 and race-3.
minors.suggested_fix: After promote, print 'fork directory kept at <path> — `asp discard <fork>` removes it'; add `asp discard race-2` to the quickstart (or a note); document the sibling `dir@fork` location in README/FAQ. Consider listing promoted-but-present forks in `asp forks` with a 'promoted' status column.

minors.title: `asp doctor` recommends --fix for findings --fix cannot repair, and exits 0 on error-severity findings
minors.description: With a CRC-corrupt journal line (non-tail corruption, exactly the case format.md says doctor must surface), `asp doctor` prints the error plus 'run asp doctor --fix to apply safe repairs'; running `asp doctor --fix` prints the identical error with no [fixed] marker and no further guidance — a dead-end loop where the corrective hint corrects nothing. Both invocations exit 0, so scripts/CI cannot detect an unhealthy store without parsing output, which sits oddly with the agent-first/--json design.
minors.evidence: Reproduced by inserting a bad-CRC line into .asp/journal.jsonl: `asp doctor` → 'error: journal line 2 is corrupt (CRC mismatch)... run asp doctor --fix to apply safe repairs' (exit 0); `asp doctor --fix` → same error, nothing fixed (exit 0). The suggestion at main.rs:657 fires whenever any finding is unfixed, regardless of fixability.
minors.suggested_fix: Track fixability per finding; only suggest --fix when a fixable finding exists, and give unfixable findings their own corrective text (e.g. 'provenance for that entry is lost; checkpoints are unaffected — remove the line to silence this'). Exit nonzero (or add --exit-code) when error-severity findings remain.

minors.title: Restore with a path not in the checkpoint leaks a raw git error with an irrelevant hint
minors.description: `asp restore 1 no/such/file` produces 'error: git checkout-index failed: git checkout-index: no/such/file is not in the cache' with 'hint: run `asp doctor` to check workspace health'. The workspace is healthy; the path just isn't in that checkpoint. The hint sends users (and agents, which the project promises can self-correct from hints) down a dead end. Every other error path I triggered (18 total) had accurate corrective hints — this is the one regression from that standard.
minors.evidence: Observed: 'error: git checkout-index failed: git checkout-index: no/such/file is not in the cache / hint: run `asp doctor` to check workspace health' (generic GitFailed hint applied to a user-input error).
minors.suggested_fix: Before checkout-index, validate requested paths against the checkpoint tree (git ls-tree) and return a dedicated error: "path 'no/such/file' is not in checkpoint #1 — hint: `asp diff 1` lists the files it contains".

minors.title: `asp setup claude --remove` creates .claude/settings.json and .mcp.json in projects that never had them
minors.description: setup_claude writes both files unconditionally even on remove: in a project with no Claude integration, `asp setup claude --remove` creates `.claude/settings.json` containing `{}` and `.mcp.json` containing `{}` — an uninstall that installs files. Similarly, removing a real install leaves `.mcp.json` as `{"mcpServers": {}}` rather than deleting the now-empty file. Removal of actual hook entries is otherwise exemplary (user hooks and settings preserved, verified).
minors.evidence: Reproduced in a fresh `asp init` directory with no .claude/.mcp.json: after `asp setup claude --remove`, `ls -a` shows new `.claude` and `.mcp.json` ({} contents). Code: write_json_file at hooks.rs:102 and :119 run on the remove path regardless of whether read_json_file returned None.
minors.suggested_fix: On remove, skip writing when the file did not exist; delete (or skip rewriting) files that become semantically empty ({} / empty mcpServers) after removal.

minors.title: First checkpoint of a large tree is a silent ~44s hang; README highlights omit initial-capture cost
minors.description: BENCHMARKS.md measures 'first checkpoint (sidecar active): 43.6 s' on the 100k-file/3.3GiB tree, and the .asp store lands at 3.0G. During that time asp prints nothing — no spinner, no file count (no progress code exists anywhere in the crates; spike-results.md decision #4 says 'first checkpoint shows a progress bar on big trees', which was never implemented). The README quickstart's second command is `asp checkpoint -m "baseline"` and its benchmark highlights (line 79) quote only incremental (0.7s) and no-op (~0.25s) numbers — a monorepo user's literal first command appears to hang for most of a minute, on the product whose pitch is the 100k-file monorepo.
minors.evidence: BENCHMARKS.md: 'first checkpoint (sidecar active) | 43.6 s', '.asp store size after benchmarks: 3.0G'. grep for progress/indicatif/spinner across crates/: no matches. README line 79 omits the first-capture number.
minors.suggested_fix: Print at least a one-line 'capturing N files (first checkpoint may take a while on large trees)…' before the initial capture (or a real progress bar); add the first-checkpoint cost to the README benchmark highlights and to the FAQ disk-usage answer.

minors.title: README install section omits Linux aarch64, which install.sh and the release workflow both support
minors.description: README says 'script (macOS arm64/x86_64, Linux x86_64)' but .github/workflows/release.yml builds aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu (ubuntu-24.04-arm) and install.sh maps `aarch64`+Linux to that exact asset name. Everything else in the install chain checks out (asset naming `asp-<tag>-<target>.tar.gz` identical on both sides, sha256 sidecars produced and verified, tarball layout matches the `install` invocation) — the doc just undersells a supported platform, which will turn away Graviton/Raspberry Pi/ARM-server users at the first line they read.
minors.evidence: README:90 '# script (macOS arm64/x86_64, Linux x86_64)'; release.yml matrix includes 'target: aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu / os: ubuntu-24.04-arm'; install.sh:24 'arm64|aarch64) arch="aarch64"' with Linux → unknown-linux-gnu.
minors.suggested_fix: Change the comment to '(macOS arm64/x86_64, Linux x86_64/aarch64)'.

minors.title: Hook checkpoints are skipped silently on lock contention — no retry on the advisory lock
minors.description: StoreLock::acquire uses try_lock_exclusive with no blocking or retry (store.rs:171), and `asp hook-event` swallows every error with exit 0 (hooks.rs:27-31, by design so the session never breaks). When Claude Code executes tools in parallel (or an MCP call overlaps a hook), concurrent checkpoints in the same workspace contend; the loser's checkpoint is simply skipped with only a stderr note nobody reads. Usually the next hook heals it (checkpoints capture cumulative state), but the PreToolUse-Bash safety checkpoint is exactly the one protecting against destructive commands: if it loses the lock race and the bash command then deletes files created since the last checkpoint, that work was never captured and is unrecoverable — a silent hole in the headline "bash side-effects are rewindable" promise. The Locked error's own hint says "wait for it to finish and retry", but the hook code never does.
minors.evidence: store.rs:171: `file.try_lock_exclusive().map_err(|_| Error::new(ErrorCode::Locked, "another asp process is modifying this workspace").with_hint("wait for it to finish and retry..."))` — non-blocking, no retry. hooks.rs:27-31: `if let Err(e) = try_handle_hook_event() { eprintln!("asp hook-event: {e} (session unaffected)"); }` and the process exits 0.
minors.suggested_fix: For Source::Hook (and Mcp) checkpoints, retry the lock with bounded backoff (e.g. up to ~2-5s, well inside the hook's 60s timeout), or use fs2's blocking lock_exclusive guarded by a deadline thread. A skipped pre-Bash checkpoint after retries should still not fail the session, but contention should then be rare enough not to matter.

minors.title: MCP server exits on a single invalid-UTF-8 byte on stdin
minors.description: serve() iterates `stdin.lock().lines()` and propagates the iterator's error with `let line = line?;`. BufRead::lines yields Err(InvalidData) for any non-UTF-8 line, so one bad byte from a client (or binary garbage accidentally piped in) kills the whole server — every queued and future request in the session is dropped — instead of answering with a JSON-RPC -32700 parse error and continuing, which is exactly what the loop already does for non-UTF-8-but-invalid JSON.
minors.evidence: Reproduced: piping `{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"initialize",...}\n{"bad \xff utf8"}\n{...ping...}` into `asp mcp` → initialize is answered, then "error: mcp server I/O error: stream did not contain valid UTF-8", exit code 1; the ping is never answered.
minors.suggested_fix: Read raw lines with read_until(b'\n') and parse with serde_json::from_slice (or from_utf8_lossy); on failure reply with the existing -32700 parse-error response and continue the loop. Only exit on a real EOF/IO error from stdin.

minors.title: run_with_stdin: early git exit during stdin write loses git's stderr and leaks a zombie child
minors.description: run_with_stdin writes the whole input with write_all before waiting. If git exits early (e.g. update-index --index-info hitting a malformed line, or add --pathspec-from-file erroring) while asp is still writing a large input (pathspec lists can approach the 2000-path threshold), write_all fails with EPIPE and `?` returns a bare "I/O error: Broken pipe" — git's actual stderr (the corrective information the trust model promises) is never collected, and the Child is dropped without wait(), leaving a zombie. Zombies are harmless in the short-lived CLI but accumulate in the long-lived `asp mcp` server process. (Checked the classic stdout-buffer deadlock too: the commands used — hash-object --stdin, update-index --index-info, add --pathspec-from-file — consume all stdin before producing output, so deadlock is not reachable today; the EPIPE path is the live defect.)
minors.evidence: gitx.rs:88-93: `child.stdin.as_mut().expect("piped stdin").write_all(input.as_bytes())?;` — on Err the function returns before `child.wait_with_output()` (line 93), so stderr is lost and the child is never reaped.
minors.suggested_fix: Capture the write result instead of `?`-ing it: ignore ErrorKind::BrokenPipe, drop the stdin handle, always call wait_with_output(), and report git's exit status + stderr (appending a note about the write failure for non-EPIPE errors). That both reaps the child and restores the corrective error text.

minors.title: btrfs CI job never asserts reflink actually happened — silent degradation to byte-copy would pass
minors.description: The `linux-reflink` job correctly mounts btrfs and routes test workspaces onto it via TMPDIR (the tempfile crate honors TMPDIR, verified semantics), so the FICLONE code path is executed. But no test in the workspace asserts `CloneMethod::Reflink`: fork.rs's own test explicitly discards the method on non-macOS (`#[cfg(not(target_os = "macos"))] let _ = method;`), and no CLI/torture test checks the reported method either. If the FICLONE call regressed (wrong ioctl, EPERM, fd mixup) every file would silently fall back to `std::fs::copy`, the job would stay green, and the headline "O(1) fork" claim would be quietly false on Linux — the job is named "exercises FICLONE reflink forks" but proves only that the code doesn't crash on btrfs.
minors.evidence: ci.yml:49 `run: TMPDIR=/mnt/btrfs cargo test --workspace` with no reflink-specific test selection; fork.rs:167-170 discards `method` on Linux; `grep -rn Reflink crates/*/tests/` returns nothing.
minors.suggested_fix: Add an env-gated assertion: in the fork test (or a dedicated integration test), when `ASP_TEST_EXPECT_REFLINK=1` is set, assert `clone_tree` returns `CloneMethod::Reflink` (and optionally that a forked `asp fork --json` reports `"method":"reflink"`); set that variable in the btrfs job. Guard with a statfs check that TMPDIR is really btrfs so the assertion is meaningful.

minors.title: Linux walk applies source directory permissions before populating children — read-only dirs in the tree abort the fork mid-walk
minors.description: reflink_walk (and copy_recursive, used as the macOS ENOTSUP and non-unix fallback) does `create_dir(dst)` and immediately `set_permissions(dst, src_permissions)` before creating any children. If a directory in the tree is read-only (mode 555 — Go module caches (`GOFLAGS`/in-tree GOPATH produce 555 dirs), some bazel outputs, or anything an agent `chmod -R a-w`'d), every subsequent `File::create`/`create_dir` inside it fails EACCES, the fork errors mid-walk, and — per the torn-fork finding — leaves an undetectable partial clone. macOS clonefile handles this in-kernel, so the macOS test matrix never sees it. `cp -R` and rsync solve this by deferring directory permission application until after the directory is populated.
minors.evidence: fork.rs:86-89: `std::fs::create_dir(dst)?; if let Ok(md) = std::fs::metadata(src) { let _ = std::fs::set_permissions(dst, md.permissions()); }` runs before the `for entry in read_dir(src)` loop that creates children; same ordering in copy_recursive at fork.rs:125-128. A 555 source dir therefore yields a 555 destination dir that children are then created inside.
minors.suggested_fix: Create directories user-writable, recurse, then apply the source permissions on the way out of the recursion (post-order), matching cp -R semantics. Add a Linux test with a `chmod 555` subdirectory in the source tree asserting fork succeeds and the clone's dir mode matches.

summaries.summary: Data-safety/engine-correctness review of asp v0.1.0. The small-file core (checkpoint/restore/fork/journal/promote plumbing, kill-9 ordering for refs+journal, torn-tail healing, fork crash detection) is genuinely solid and matches the format doc. The large-blob sidecar and its interactions are where the trust model breaks, and the existing suites (all green) cover none of it. Two release blockers: (1) a big file that shrinks below the threshold is silently recorded as deleted and `asp restore` of that checkpoint then deletes it from disk — pure data loss, no crash required (workspace.rs:596); (2) `asp promote` commits the fork's entire .asp store — shadow git objects, journal, CAS blobs — into the user's branch, bloating the repo and leaking the full private edit history if pushed (workspace.rs:1566). Four major engine bugs, all reproduced byte-for-byte in /tmp with the release binary: kill -9 windows around the manifest (meta ref written after checkpoint ref; checkout-index before CAS materialization) leave pointer-JSON files that the next checkpoint legitimizes while doctor reports healthy; the no-op fast path returns the wrong tree after a big-file-only restore (post-restore checkpoint skipped, `asp diff` gives inverted answers); `asp status` is blind to big-file edits so undo restores the wrong checkpoint; and repeated `asp undo` ping-pongs between the last two states, never reaching older history. Minor: fork_compare/discard mutate a fork's store without its lock (contradicts format.md's locking claim; demonstrated), journal self-heal truncation runs on lock-free read paths, and non-UTF-8 filenames brick checkpointing on Linux. Suggested fixes are concrete and small; the big-file fixes plus a big-file kill-9 torture extension would close most of the gap.
asp v0.1.0 Security review: 2 release-blocking findings (PoCs in findings).

I built the release binary, followed README quickstart literally in fresh /tmp projects, exercised every command, 18 error paths, the MCP server, the Claude Code hooks files, and the stock-git recovery runbook. Much is genuinely solid: install.sh asset naming exactly matches the release workflow (including sha256 sidecars); the recovery runbook in docs/design/format.md works verbatim; the >50MB blob sidecar round-trips byte-identically; `.mcp.json` and `.claude/settings.json` are valid current Claude Code formats, installed idempotently and removed without clobbering user hooks/settings; the MCP server answers initialize/tools/list/tools/call with exactly the tools the README names; most error hints are excellent (init twice, lock contention, promote without git, bad checkpoint refs); `cargo test --workspace` passes. But I found one launch-blocking bug and two major product/docs gaps, all in the first five minutes of the advertised hero workflows: (1) `asp promote` commits the fork's entire `.asp/` store (shadow git repo, journal, lock) into the user's branch, and the merge command asp itself prints then fails on untracked-file collisions — the flagship fork→promote→merge loop is broken out of the box; (2) repeated `asp undo` oscillates between the last two states (undo of the undo) and can never reach older checkpoints — with history v1→v2→v3, undo×2 lands you back on v3; (3) checkpoints silently exclude gitignored files, so the README's named example (".env") is not protected by undo in the common case where .env is gitignored. Plus nine smaller error-quality/docs-accuracy issues including two literal un-interpolated `{fork_name}` placeholders in hints — notable because corrective hints are the stated brand.

Reviewed crates/asp-core and crates/asp for Rust robustness ahead of v0.1.0. The foundations are genuinely solid — CRC journal with torn-tail recovery, atomic tmp+rename writes everywhere, the kill -9 torture suite and property tests all pass, error hints are consistently good, and the porcelain -z parsing is bounds-checked (no reachable panics found in production code). However, I found one critical and five major launch-relevant defects, all verified empirically or grounded in exact code paths. Critical: `asp promote` commits the entire .asp store (shadow.git objects, journal, lock, CAS blobs) onto the user's branch because build_user_commit stages with plain `git add -A .` and nothing ever excludes .asp from the user repo — the README's own `git merge asp/<fork>` follow-up then fails on .asp collisions (reproduced end to end; the CI test never asserts .asp absence). Major: (1) `asp doctor --fix` remove_dir_all's any `<dir>@<name>` sibling it misjudges as a torn fork — reproduced deleting a user's `cp -r` backup containing new work; (2) non-UTF-8 filenames (legal on Linux, a launch platform) make every checkpoint fail via from_utf8_lossy-corrupted pathspecs, and the hook wrapper swallows the failure so auto-checkpointing silently stops; (3) fork_compare runs stage_tree on forks without their lock, mutating their index/excludes and racing in-fork checkpoints — reproduced raw git index.lock errors whose advice ("remove the file manually") is actively harmful; (4) Journal::read() self-heal truncates the journal on lock-free read paths (every open/status/log/MCP call), able to chop a concurrently fsynced entry, contradicting format.md's "reads are lock-free"; (5) store_in_cas hashes before cloning, so a big file written during checkpoint poisons an immutable CAS entry and silently breaks byte-identical restore. Minor: hook checkpoints skip silently on lock contention (no retry, weakens the pre-Bash safety net under parallel tool use), the MCP server exits entirely on one invalid-UTF-8 stdin byte (reproduced), and run_with_stdin loses git's stderr and leaks a zombie child on EPIPE (no actual pipe-buffer deadlock is reachable with the git commands used). Each finding includes a concrete, small fix; the promote leak and doctor --fix deletion are the two I would block the launch on.

Cross-platform/environment review of agentspaces v0.1.0. The core shadow-git design is solid where it pins things (explicit GIT_DIR bypasses safe.directory, so sudo/mixed-ownership stores keep working; no shell interpolation, so spaces/unicode workspace paths are fine; the btrfs CI job's TMPDIR really does route tempfile workspaces onto btrfs; the stock-git recovery runbook is genuinely tested in engine.rs:373; full suite passes locally). But I found 8 real issues, 6 of them major: (1) the released Linux binaries are glibc-2.39-dynamic, not the advertised "static binary" — they will not start on Ubuntu 22.04/Debian 12/RHEL 9/Alpine; (2) the documented kill-9 fork invariant is false on Linux: a clone that dies before `.asp` is copied is invisible to `asp doctor`, unremovable via asp, and permanently blocks the fork name (demonstrated); (3) promote's user-git path inherits GIT_DIR/GIT_WORK_TREE from the environment — with an exported absolute GIT_DIR (dotfiles pattern, hook environments) promote writes objects/refs into an unrelated repo and fails (demonstrated); (4) relative `-C`/MCP `directory` paths break every shadow git call with a misleading "run asp doctor" hint (demonstrated); (5) one non-UTF8 filename (possible on Linux) makes every checkpoint fail with exit 128, silently disabling the auto-checkpoint safety net (failure mode demonstrated); (6) the "git ≥ 2.30" claim is wrong — config isolation depends on GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL/GIT_CONFIG_SYSTEM, which git only honors from 2.32, so on Debian 11 / RHEL 9.0 user global config (core.autocrlf etc.) silently leaks into the shadow store; there is also no runtime git-version check at all. Two minor: the btrfs CI job never asserts reflink was actually used, and the Linux walk chmods directories read-only before filling them, so read-only dirs in the tree abort forks mid-walk.
